--begin quoted text:

I am not sure if this is the cause or it is the difference between 'if it 
ain't broke, don't fix it' and 'let's take it apart to see how it works!'

A buddy o' mine used to be Materials/Inventory Manager for a jewelery 
manufacturer... he could never understand folks who considered inertia to 
be valuable, in and of it'sself.  After a meeting where things got a 
bit... heated a VP took him aside and asked, seriously, what the problem 
was... after all, in his (the VP's) department they'd been doing things 
exactly the same way for the past twenty years.

'Where's your passion for work?', asked my buddy, 'Where do you strive to 
do something more, where to you work to make things better?'

'You don't understand', said the VP, 'we've been doing the exact same 
thing for the past twenty years.'

The VP could not understand why anyone would not see this as a Very Good 
Thing.

--end quoted text

... and as for the EVALUATE, the abovecited posting contains a link to 
*another* posting... which mentions a 'frangipani-wrapped bedrool' (should 
have been 'bedroll') and a plea made to Mr Standards-and-Practises 
Reviewmeister.

…[5 more quoted lines elided]…

and, more to the point would it be faster than a plain old binary
search given the overhead of doing all this.  

>The only reason I can think of NOT to use a SEARCH ALL when the various
>syntactic elements allow it is when the programmer has reason to believe the

data and inconsistent results are not acceptable.

```

"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote in message
news:d7i8cp$24rv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com...
> Howard Brazee wrote:
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> SEARCH ALL are very tightly restricted (the WHEN clause, if not
referencing
> an 88-level item, is limited in its subjects to keys referenced in the KEY
> IS phrase of OCCURS).  It MAY well be possible for the implementor to
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> But again, *any* difference in the "execution-time resource consumption"
of
> a SEARCH ALL as compared to an identical SEARCH is entirely up to the
> implementor.   The implementation programmer *may* or *may not* experience
a
> performance difference between them.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> unconditionally returned -- that is, the "found entry" for SEARCH and
SEARCH
> ALL was not always and unconditionally the same when there were duplicates
> in the table.  That both the documentation and the standard at the time
> specified that which entry was returned was undefined in such a case was
not
> deemed a sufficient argument by the product's users; the user base
demanded
> *identical* answers no matter whether there were duplicate keys, or indeed
> no matter whether the keys were in any sort of order at all.  A serial
> search meets the criteria specified by the standard in such a case; in
this
> instance, the serial search *also* met the needs of the product's actual
> customers better than the *binary* search did.   The implementors tried to
> convince the customer base that it was the user's responsibility, not the
> compiler's, to ensure that the keys were in order, but did not succeed;
the
> reality of politics between customer and vendor outranked any academe's
> thoughts as to The Way Things Ought To Be.
>
>     -Chuck Stevens
>
I followed your very lucid arguments in this thread, Chuck, and I take your
point about standards and expectations.

The above anecdote amused me because it is SIMPLE to extend the binary chop
so that it DOES always return a consistent result when there are duplicate
keys. (You can have the first through the nth duplicate always returned, by
adding a few lines of code that STILL make it more efficient than a serial
search, everything else being equal.)

If I was a vendor with a multi-million dollar sale hanging on this, I'd be
inclined to give the customer what they wanted, rather than taking the party
line that "that's the way it is". It means a simple PTF for that site only.
Not difficult to administer or maintain. I suspect what stopped this was not
the rights or wrongs of the requirement, but the inabilityof the implementor
to deliver because of the internal politics required to be overcome in order
to provide the requested solution.

I worked many years ago for a household name computer corporation that I
won't mention here. My job was 'pre-sales', supporting one of the company's
hotshot salesmen and making sure our proposals were technically viable. The
salesman concerned was extremely successful and one of the best I have ever
seen. (I learned much from him about presentation and attitude, even though
the technical nature of my role didn't really require these things...(you
either knew your stuff or you didn't. If you didn't, you weren't there very
long..)).

He decided to sell some inventory that was around 1000 CRT units.  The
company was offering a bonus to get them moved and he reckoned he could get
a foot in the door at a prestige site if he flogged these terminals to them.
I went with an engineer and we connected one of them to the prospect's
equipment, as a trial. It blew the port. The engineer, put a scope on the
interface and tried again on another port. It blew that one too, but before
it did we gathered enough data to realise that the host interpretation of
the RS232C standard was different from our interpretaion of it...

I duly reported back to the salesman  (my Boss) that it was impossible to
connect our stuff to their stuff.  That was the man part of my job; ensuring
we configured things correctly and everything worked, both hardware and
software.

I heard no more about it until  a few weeks later, when someone said there
were a lot of cartons coming into the warehouse. Sure enough, it was the
CRTs. In horror I went to my Boss again and asked if he knew about this. He
said he did and had signed a deal with the prospect for all of them. I asked
about the little problem of them not being compatible and he smiled, sat me
down, and told me the following:

Although I have quoted this, it is not verbatim. It is as close as I can
remember after nearly 30 years... it is a fair and accurate statement of his
thoughts and actions...
"If you really want to do something, don't let perceived wisdom stop you. I
got on the phone to one of our manufacturing facilities in the US and
requested a board be produced that could correct the voltage
incompatibilities you identified in your report. The plant manager asked how
many we needed and I told him 1000. He laughed. It would work out uneconomic
to set up a run for that quantity, even if we could get permission to have
them designed and prototyped. The units would cost at least $3 dollars each
just for the parts. The design and build would take at least 3 months,
assuming all necessary paperwork was in order and it could be fitted into
the current work schedule. Not a chance. I thanked him and rang X [The
President of the Company] I explained that this is an important account in a
market we are looking to develop and penetrate, and that the prospect is
currently solid with our opposition. I spent a few weeks on his yacht
cruising with him after I won the "Salesman of the Year" award a couple of
years ago (before volunteering to come here and establish a presence for
us), and he knows how serious I am about our company. We got the boards we
need and they are being delivered with the terminals. The whole process took
four weeks. I didn't tell you because I wanted to surprise you (he certainly
did that; I was stunned...). It is amazing what people can do when they are
motivated. A call or visit from X tends to be very motivating..."

Sure enough, there was a tiny black box that sat between the terminal and
the host, and everything worked. We later sold $10,000,000 of services and
equipment to that client and the opposition salesman responsible for the
account was fired.

The point about this story is that the attitude of people (whether technical
or sales) can make all the difference. I wrote it off when I saw it didn't
work. My Boss simply would NOT write off and decided it HAD to work. (Guess
which of us is more wealthy...<G>?)

It was youthful experiences like this that shaped the incredibly capable and
positive consultant you see today...:-) (bows, and exits stage right,
stumbling over long cape...)

Pete.
```

"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3g7ahuFaeu4lU1@individual.net...
> The above anecdote amused me because it is SIMPLE to extend the binary
chop
> so that it DOES always return a consistent result when there are duplicate
> keys. (You can have the first through the nth duplicate always returned,
by
> adding a few lines of code that STILL make it more efficient than a serial
> search, everything else being equal.)

The very idea that a 'consistent' result should be 'expected' when doing a
keyed search on any collection of records with duplicate keys suggests it's
time for a return trip to the drawing board.

Sure, one could manufacture a case in which a binary search (by whatever
means) followed by a sequential search using as a starting point the ordinal
position returned by a 'pure' binary search (e.g., find 'nth' key in ordinal
sequence of table entries with duplicate keys) will 'outperform' a pure
linear  search; but if I found myself doing that I'd like to think I'd
recognize this as an opportunity to make that return trip.

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: SEARCH ALL

_(thread depth: 20)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2005-06-02T10:25:58-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d7nff6$2735$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<1116967937.156087.198120@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <d704ka$bth$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <lj6791tjgr1q5n43e8ll3cfr4v7k0l7urf@4ax.com> <d720n3$ku8$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <gkf991t55u3mg8n1nrmkvfh8kaogk4s5l6@4ax.com> <d72ga0$94b$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <jpm991922leaap63fquei82jm2lno88c44@4ax.com> <119a8g5hjc729a4@news.supernews.com> <j71b9196v3g2uhuq2tn3r7397bd5n4dib0@4ax.com> <119daflcv5nqt7d@news.supernews.com> <d77er5$1pko$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <119frrqikne09a0@news.supernews.com> <d7i0ji$20c0$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <d7i67s$fp8$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <d7i8cp$24rv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3g7ahuFaeu4lU1@individual.net>`

```

"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3g7ahuFaeu4lU1@individual.net...

> The above anecdote amused me because it is SIMPLE to extend the binary
chop
> so that it DOES always return a consistent result when there are duplicate
> keys.

Of course it is.  And the user certainly is now, and always has been, free
to incorporate such techniques into his programs at the source level.

> (You can have the first through the nth duplicate always returned, by
> adding a few lines of code that STILL make it more efficient than a serial
> search, everything else being equal.)

The problem is determining whether or not "everything else being equal"
applies to all comparisons between SEARCH and SEARCH ALL for all users in
all applications.  What criteria should the *compiler* apply unconditionally
to make that determination, based on the fact that the efficacy of a binary
search is entirely dependent on the correct ordering of the data, over which
the compiler has no control?

> If I was a vendor with a multi-million dollar sale hanging on this, I'd be
> inclined to give the customer what they wanted, rather than taking the
party
> line that "that's the way it is".

Which is pretty much what happened.  Optimal performance for a given
construct is a goal, not necessarily a requirement -- many is the time I've
had to correct problems in which the compiler generated code that produced
the wrong answers very quickly so that it produced the right answers,
perhaps rather less quickly.  We had a number of powerful users complaining
that they didn't get exactly the same results with SEARCH ALL that they were
getting with SEARCH, and they quite happily accepted the use of a serial
SEARCH for SEARCH ALL as a solution to *their* problem; we had a few queries
from customers who thought the performance of SEARCH ALL could benefit from
some tweaking, but in a choice between a wrong answer arrived at quickly and
a correct answer arrived at rather less quickly, we picked the latter for
our COBOL74 implementation.

Note that Unisys MCP COBOL85 does indeed do a binary search for SEARCH ALL;
users who want the behavior of SEARCH without ALL in this context (in
particular in the case of duplicate or out-of-order keys) are encouraged to
use an otherwise-identical SEARCH instead of SEARCH ALL.

> It means a simple PTF for that site only.

I don't know the acronym PTF, but I can say that it would be rather far from
simple either to develop or to maintain a change to the Unisys MCP COBOL74
compiler to meet these customers' (note the plural) expectation of
unconditionally-identical results with SEARCH and SEARCH ALL.

> Not difficult to administer or maintain. I suspect what stopped this was
not
> the rights or wrongs of the requirement, but the inabilityof the
implementor
> to deliver because of the internal politics required to be overcome in
order
> to provide the requested solution.

The requested solution was "We want SEARCH ALL unconditionally to produce
the same table-element as SEARCH, even when the SEARCH ALL results are
undefined".  We provided that solution, and we provided it to *all* our
customers because we believed that's what at least the *vocal* majority
wanted.  With later compilers we convinced them that if those are the
results they wanted they should use SEARCH, and restrict SEARCH ALL to those
cases in which the *standard* defines the results.

> I worked many years ago for a household name computer corporation that I
> won't mention here. My job was 'pre-sales', supporting one of the
company's
> hotshot salesmen and making sure our proposals were technically viable.
...

Not sure that particular example is entirely comparable, though.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

Let's just get the following added to the standard, following the
'restrictions/comments' following the syntax diagram,OK?

"If you don't understand this, "SEARCH ALL may not be used."

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: SEARCH ALL

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3g9qfdFauokoU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<1116967937.156087.198120@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <d704ka$bth$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <lj6791tjgr1q5n43e8ll3cfr4v7k0l7urf@4ax.com> <d720n3$ku8$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <gkf991t55u3mg8n1nrmkvfh8kaogk4s5l6@4ax.com> <d72ga0$94b$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <jpm991922leaap63fquei82jm2lno88c44@4ax.com> <119a8g5hjc729a4@news.supernews.com> <j71b9196v3g2uhuq2tn3r7397bd5n4dib0@4ax.com> <119daflcv5nqt7d@news.supernews.com> <d77er5$1pko$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <119frrqikne09a0@news.supernews.com> <d7i0ji$20c0$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <d7i67s$fp8$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <d7i8cp$24rv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3g7ahuFaeu4lU1@individual.net> <d7nff6$2735$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```

"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote in message
news:d7nff6$2735$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com...
>
> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> > so that it DOES always return a consistent result when there are
duplicate
> > keys.
>
> Of course it is.  And the user certainly is now, and always has been, free
> to incorporate such techniques into his programs at the source level.
>

For several million dollars, I'd have done it for him :-)

> > (You can have the first through the nth duplicate always returned, by
> > adding a few lines of code that STILL make it more efficient than a
serial
> > search, everything else being equal.)
>
> The problem is determining whether or not "everything else being equal"
> applies to all comparisons between SEARCH and SEARCH ALL for all users in
> all applications.

No, I agree that is not feasible. My mail was about ONE potential customer.

PTF is 'Program Temporary Fix'

IBM send these out to sites where they are applied by System Programmers to
IBM software as urgent fixes, pending the next full release.

I'm not sure whether this still happens, but in the days when I was a System
Programmer these things arrived on mag tape and were applied as soon as
possible. Sites who complain about bugs they have found will be offered a
PTF tape. Sometimes, even if you have no complaints, but IBM realise the
software is flawed, you will receive a PTF tape anyway. (Whether or not you
apply it is up to you...). Installations keep track of which PTFs have been
applied. When the new release comes out, it usually incorporates the fixes
so the PTFs are then redundant.

The point is that this is a standard mechanism that allows variations in
site software. So it could accommodate the 'tailoring' of a given piece of
software for a given site. It isn't something that the vendor would WANT to
do as a matter of course, but that was what prompted my story about how far
you go with sales...
<snip>

> I don't know the acronym PTF, but I can say that it would be rather far
from
> simple either to develop or to maintain a change to the Unisys MCP COBOL74
> compiler to meet these customers' (note the plural) expectation of
> unconditionally-identical results with SEARCH and SEARCH ALL.
>
See above.

<snip>
>
> Not sure that particular example is entirely comparable, though.
>
There were several points in the story that would apply to what we are
discussing.

1. Should something not be delivered because the technical people don't like
it? (Sometimes the tech vision is limited...sometimes it isn't.)
2. Should internal politics be allowed to diminish customer service?(It
really depends on what is at stake...and the impacts on other customers)
3.  Is it right that Sales should have more power than Technical Services,
within the organization? (While Technical Services is essential for proper
functioning of the company's products, without Sales there is no
company....deal with it :-))

It was offered as an example of what can drive sales and promote a culture
of customer service. I found it illuminating at the time and it has stayed
with me ever since.

Finally, there was no criticism, expressed or implied, of Unisys or any
other vendor.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: SEARCH ALL

_(thread depth: 22)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2005-06-03T09:35:38-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d7q0sr$lt0$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<1116967937.156087.198120@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <d704ka$bth$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <lj6791tjgr1q5n43e8ll3cfr4v7k0l7urf@4ax.com> <d720n3$ku8$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <gkf991t55u3mg8n1nrmkvfh8kaogk4s5l6@4ax.com> <d72ga0$94b$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <jpm991922leaap63fquei82jm2lno88c44@4ax.com> <119a8g5hjc729a4@news.supernews.com> <j71b9196v3g2uhuq2tn3r7397bd5n4dib0@4ax.com> <119daflcv5nqt7d@news.supernews.com> <d77er5$1pko$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <119frrqikne09a0@news.supernews.com> <d7i0ji$20c0$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <d7i67s$fp8$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <d7i8cp$24rv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3g7ahuFaeu4lU1@individual.net> <d7nff6$2735$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3g9qfdFauokoU1@individual.net>`

```

"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3g9qfdFauokoU1@individual.net...

> PTF is 'Program Temporary Fix'
>
> IBM send these out to sites where they are applied by System Programmers
to
> IBM software as urgent fixes, pending the next full release.

Ah.  We call those "Interim Corrections".  As patching object code is
something that is Not Done on Unisys MCP systems, the media contains a
corrected and newly-compiled version of the software.  They are available
for download by any customer as soon as the validity of the patch has been
verified by regression and unit testing.  Note that there is some risk
associated with IC's because they are not subjected either to full-bore
system "smoke" testing or to formal field testing.

> The point is that this is a standard mechanism that allows variations in
> site software. So it could accommodate the 'tailoring' of a given piece of
> software for a given site. It isn't something that the vendor would WANT
to
> do as a matter of course, but that was what prompted my story about how
far
> you go with sales...
> <snip>

This would be more along the lines of a *custom* patch for a given customer.
Although some customers do patch system software on their own, our support
policy is such that if they have done so and they encounter a problem with
patched software, the materials they provide us need to reflect the behavior
on *released* software.  A "custom" patch like we're talking about here
would more likely, in our environment, take the form of a compiler directive
that would allow the user to elect the alternative approach.


> There were several points in the story that would apply to what we are
> discussing.
>
> 1. Should something not be delivered because the technical people don't
like
> it? (Sometimes the tech vision is limited...sometimes it isn't.)

This is a case where the technical people *did* deliver something they
didn't like.   Personally, I think the users should have been informed that
SEARCH is the Right Statement to use rather than SEARCH ALL if defined
results are required when the data does not meet the requirements of SEARCH
ALL, but I wasn't involved in this decision.

> 2. Should internal politics be allowed to diminish customer service?(It
> really depends on what is at stake...and the impacts on other customers)

I don't think it applied here.  Experience has shown that those complaining
about the fact that the performance of SEARCH ALL is little different from
the performance of an equivalent SEARCH were FAR outshouted by those who
demanded that the *answers* produced by SEARCH ALL be *unconditionally* the
same for the same table contents, regardless of what the table contained.

One of the issues for the very earliest adopters of Burroughs Large Systems
COBOL74 was that they got different behavior from that compiler (which for a
time actually did have a binary search for SEARCH ALL) from that which they
observed with COBOL(68), and they felt the behavior should be
Unconditionally Identical, and migratability and portability were and are
Major Concerns.   The earliest adopters of COBOL74 held a whole lot of clout
in determining direction for the product.

It was indeed a political issue; the technical folk wanted to provide the
execution-time performance of a binary search for SEARCH ALL; the management
felt that, despite the caveats in the standards and in the documentation
about undefined results, the undefined results needed to be the same when
migrating from COBOL(68) to COBOL74.   We managed to convince them otherwise
in the case of  COBOL85.

> 3.  Is it right that Sales should have more power than Technical Services,
> within the organization? (While Technical Services is essential for proper
> functioning of the company's products, without Sales there is no
> company....deal with it :-))

A customer may indeed convince the sales force that the sum of 1 and 2
should be 47.  The sales force can certainly put pressure on Technical
Services to make it *possible* for that to be the case for those customers
that desire that it be so, but I think it's a disservice to the customer
base at large to demand that it be so by default.

In the case of SEARCH ALL, the best way to make the statement
unconditionally produce the same *answer* as SEARCH is to replace "ALL" with
spaces in the program source.  Degrading the performance of SEARCH ALL for
all customers is a decision with which I disagree, largely because adding a
Compiler Directive to a program to tell it to use a serial search for SEARCH
ALL is likely to be more trouble for the user than deleting the word "ALL".
The problem here is that the customer demanded that they not be required to
make *any* change *at all* for COBOL74 to behave the same in this respect as
COBOL(68).

> Finally, there was no criticism, expressed or implied, of Unisys or any
> other vendor.

Understood; I didn't take it that way.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
Pete Dashwood wrote:
> "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote in message
> news:d7nff6$2735$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com...
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> software for a given site. ...
Right on, Pete. In fact, I seldom run into sites that are identical in 
what they have applied. The "unsolicited" PTF tapes contain just about 
everything IBM has come up with since the last one, so some of the 
contents won't even apply to systems that aren't using some products. 
After that, there is a fair amount of "ain't broke, don't fix" logic, 
wading through the explanations of what the PTF does and deciding if the 
local system already has or is on a collision course with the bug; if 
not, why spend the effort to apply code that might uncover something 
ugly. There is also a tendency of many old SPs to let the other guy 
smoke test a new the fix. Often a conservative shop will deliberately 
stay a couple of PTF tapes behind.

Cutting edge is one thing, bleeding edge quite another!

Regards,
Dwight
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: SEARCH ALL

_(thread depth: 19)_

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-06-02T01:11:38-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1117699898.200346.140580@g44g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<d7i8cp$24rv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<1116967937.156087.198120@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <d704ka$bth$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <lj6791tjgr1q5n43e8ll3cfr4v7k0l7urf@4ax.com> <d720n3$ku8$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <gkf991t55u3mg8n1nrmkvfh8kaogk4s5l6@4ax.com> <d72ga0$94b$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <jpm991922leaap63fquei82jm2lno88c44@4ax.com> <119a8g5hjc729a4@news.supernews.com> <j71b9196v3g2uhuq2tn3r7397bd5n4dib0@4ax.com> <119daflcv5nqt7d@news.supernews.com> <d77er5$1pko$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <119frrqikne09a0@news.supernews.com> <d7i0ji$20c0$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <d7i67s$fp8$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <d7i8cp$24rv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```

Chuck Stevens wrote:
> I know of one implementation whose users rebelled against a pure binary
> search for SEARCH ALL because the implementor couldn't guarantee that the
> first of multiple records with a key matching the search criterion was
> unconditionally returned ...

This is easy to arrange. It only needs a 'sequence' field appended to
the key. During the load or immediately after a sort the sequence is
populated such that each unique key starts again at 1, then the SEARCH
ALL includes equaity of '1'.

If it can be done during a load in order, or by insertion, there is
almost no overhead. If the table has to be sorted then it requires a
single pass after the sort.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: SEARCH ALL

_(thread depth: 20)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2005-06-02T09:18:24-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d7nbgh$24lv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<1116967937.156087.198120@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <d704ka$bth$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <lj6791tjgr1q5n43e8ll3cfr4v7k0l7urf@4ax.com> <d720n3$ku8$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <gkf991t55u3mg8n1nrmkvfh8kaogk4s5l6@4ax.com> <d72ga0$94b$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <jpm991922leaap63fquei82jm2lno88c44@4ax.com> <119a8g5hjc729a4@news.supernews.com> <j71b9196v3g2uhuq2tn3r7397bd5n4dib0@4ax.com> <119daflcv5nqt7d@news.supernews.com> <d77er5$1pko$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <119frrqikne09a0@news.supernews.com> <d7i0ji$20c0$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <d7i67s$fp8$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <d7i8cp$24rv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <1117699898.200346.140580@g44g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`

```
I understand all of these possibilities; an *application programmer* is free
to use techniques like this.  My point is that I don't think it's
appropriate for the *standard* to impose the overhead of such techniques
onto the object code for programs that have not specified it, and I
certainly would be surprised if the *compiler* included them.

The programmer is obligated to follow the rules of SEARCH ALL -- the data is
arranged in the order of the keys, and the contents of the subject keys for
the WHEN clause are sufficient to identify uniquely the table element.  When
these rules are followed, the results of SEARCH and SEARCH ALL are the same.

The programmer is also free to do his own equivalent of SEARCH at the source
level incorporating whatever techniques he wants to accomplish whatever it
is he wants to accomplish with the data.

    -Chuck Stevens

"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:1117699898.200346.140580@g44g2000cwa.googlegroups.com...
>
> Chuck Stevens wrote:
> > I know of one implementation whose users rebelled against a pure binary
> > search for SEARCH ALL because the implementor couldn't guarantee that
the
> > first of multiple records with a key matching the search criterion was
> > unconditionally returned ...
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

On  2-Jun-2005, "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

> The programmer is obligated to follow the rules of SEARCH ALL -- the data is
> arranged in the order of the keys, and the contents of the subject keys for
> the WHEN clause are sufficient to identify uniquely the table element.  When
> these rules are followed, the results of SEARCH and SEARCH ALL are the same.

Are they the same when there are duplicate keys?

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: SEARCH ALL

_(thread depth: 22)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2005-06-02T13:11:58-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d7neku$hn5$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<1116967937.156087.198120@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <119frrqikne09a0@news.supernews.com> <d7ndks$lr$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
In article <d7ndks$lr$1@peabody.colorado.edu>,
Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
>
>On  2-Jun-2005, "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>Are they the same when there are duplicate keys?

According to 
<http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/FRAMESET/IGY3LR10/CCONTENTS?DT=20020920180651>

--begin quoted text:

The results of a SEARCH ALL operation are predictable only when: 

The data in the table is ordered in ASCENDING/DESCENDING KEY order 

The contents of the ASCENDING/DESCENDING keys specified in the WHEN clause 
provide a unique table reference. 

--end quoted text

... so it would seem that using duplicate keys, in that they do not 
provide 'a unique table reference', is not a time when '(w)hen these rules 
are followed'.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: SEARCH ALL

_(thread depth: 22)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2005-06-02T10:38:28-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d7ng6k$27ee$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<1116967937.156087.198120@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <d704ka$bth$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <lj6791tjgr1q5n43e8ll3cfr4v7k0l7urf@4ax.com> <d720n3$ku8$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <gkf991t55u3mg8n1nrmkvfh8kaogk4s5l6@4ax.com> <d72ga0$94b$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <jpm991922leaap63fquei82jm2lno88c44@4ax.com> <119a8g5hjc729a4@news.supernews.com> <j71b9196v3g2uhuq2tn3r7397bd5n4dib0@4ax.com> <119daflcv5nqt7d@news.supernews.com> <d77er5$1pko$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <119frrqikne09a0@news.supernews.com> <d7i0ji$20c0$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <d7i67s$fp8$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <d7i8cp$24rv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <1117699898.200346.140580@g44g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <d7nbgh$24lv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <d7ndks$lr$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
The standards rules are the same for duplicate keys *so long as you're not
trying to match one of the duplicates*.

If you are, and you're using SEARCH, the search index will point to the
first of the matching entries because the search is serial.  If you're using
SEARCH ALL, the results are undefined.

    -Chuck Stevens

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:d7ndks$lr$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> On  2-Jun-2005, "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
>
> > The programmer is obligated to follow the rules of SEARCH ALL -- the
data is
> > arranged in the order of the keys, and the contents of the subject keys
for
> > the WHEN clause are sufficient to identify uniquely the table element.
When
> > these rules are followed, the results of SEARCH and SEARCH ALL are the
same.
>
> Are they the same when there are duplicate keys?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: SEARCH ALL

_(thread depth: 24)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2005-06-02T12:25:35-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d7nmfg$2b74$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<1116967937.156087.198120@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <d704ka$bth$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <lj6791tjgr1q5n43e8ll3cfr4v7k0l7urf@4ax.com> <d720n3$ku8$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <gkf991t55u3mg8n1nrmkvfh8kaogk4s5l6@4ax.com> <d72ga0$94b$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <jpm991922leaap63fquei82jm2lno88c44@4ax.com> <119a8g5hjc729a4@news.supernews.com> <j71b9196v3g2uhuq2tn3r7397bd5n4dib0@4ax.com> <119daflcv5nqt7d@news.supernews.com> <d77er5$1pko$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <119frrqikne09a0@news.supernews.com> <d7i0ji$20c0$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <d7i67s$fp8$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <d7i8cp$24rv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <1117699898.200346.140580@g44g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <d7nbgh$24lv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <d7ndks$lr$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <d7ng6k$27ee$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <d7nl2o$i05$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:d7nl2o$i05$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> On  2-Jun-2005, "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
>
> > > > these rules are followed, the results of SEARCH and SEARCH ALL are
the
> > same.
> > >
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> > The standards rules are the same for duplicate keys *so long as you're
not
> > trying to match one of the duplicates*.
> >
> > If you are, and you're using SEARCH, the search index will point to the
> > first of the matching entries because the search is serial.  If you're
using
> > SEARCH ALL, the results are undefined.
>
> Which means "no".

Let's try this again.

The rules for SEARCH ALL say the results are predictable *only* when (1)
the data is ordered according to the ASCENDING/DESCENDING key clause; and
(2) the contents of the key are sufficient to identify a unique table
element.   The '02 standard goes further:  "At the start of the execution of
a SEARCH statement with the ALL phrase specified, the following conditions
SHALL BE true: ..." (emphasis added).

When you follow the rules for SEARCH ALL, the table is in order, and there
may be no more than one entry in the table that satisfies the WHEN criteria.

When you follow the rules for SEARCH ALL*, the results of a SEARCH ALL are
*the same* as the results of a SEARCH using the same WHEN criteria.  You
either find the item if it's there, or you don't if it isn't.

When you *do not* follow the rules for SEARCH ALL*, the result of execution
of that SEARCH ALL is undefined, which is different from the result of a
SEARCH with the same WHEN criteria.  But that only occurs if you have *not*
complied with the documented requirements for SEARCH ALL.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: SEARCH ALL

_(thread depth: 25)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2005-06-02T20:06:41+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d7nosg$nc9$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<1116967937.156087.198120@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <d704ka$bth$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <lj6791tjgr1q5n43e8ll3cfr4v7k0l7urf@4ax.com> <d720n3$ku8$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <gkf991t55u3mg8n1nrmkvfh8kaogk4s5l6@4ax.com> <d72ga0$94b$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <jpm991922leaap63fquei82jm2lno88c44@4ax.com> <119a8g5hjc729a4@news.supernews.com> <j71b9196v3g2uhuq2tn3r7397bd5n4dib0@4ax.com> <119daflcv5nqt7d@news.supernews.com> <d77er5$1pko$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <119frrqikne09a0@news.supernews.com> <d7i0ji$20c0$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <d7i67s$fp8$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <d7i8cp$24rv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <1117699898.200346.140580@g44g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <d7nbgh$24lv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <d7ndks$lr$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <d7nmfg$2b74$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```

On  2-Jun-2005, "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

> > Which means "no".
>
> Let's try this again.

No, I understood you the first time.   My objection was with the following
statement:

> > > > > these rules are followed, the results of SEARCH and SEARCH ALL are
> > > > > the same.

It's obvious that if there is one match, and you code correctly, both will come
up with that match.

The only time this statement is meaningful is when there are multiple correct
match.   In that case we cannot count on SEARCH and SEARCH ALL coming up with
the same match.    Now the statement is meaningful - but wrong.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: SEARCH ALL

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:d7nosg$nc9$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> On  2-Jun-2005, "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > > > > these rules are followed, the results of SEARCH and SEARCH ALL
are
> > > > > > the same.
>
> It's obvious that if there is one match, and you code correctly, both will
come
> up with that match.
>
> The only time this statement is meaningful is when there are multiple
correct
> match.   In that case we cannot count on SEARCH and SEARCH ALL coming up
with
> the same match.    Now the statement is meaningful - but wrong.

No, because if that's the circumstance you find yourself in, you have *not*
followed the rules for SEARCH ALL.  The fact that you have multiple table
elements that match the key is itself a violation of the general rules for
that statement.  This is clarified even further in ISO/IEC 1989:2002.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

- **From:** Scott Peterson <scottp4.removethistoreply@mindspring.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<119t6qu2uhajv46@news.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<d704ka$bth$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <lj6791tjgr1q5n43e8ll3cfr4v7k0l7urf@4ax.com> <d720n3$ku8$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <gkf991t55u3mg8n1nrmkvfh8kaogk4s5l6@4ax.com> <d72ga0$94b$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <jpm991922leaap63fquei82jm2lno88c44@4ax.com> <119a8g5hjc729a4@news.supernews.com> <j71b9196v3g2uhuq2tn3r7397bd5n4dib0@4ax.com> <119daflcv5nqt7d@news.supernews.com> <d77er5$1pko$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <119frrqikne09a0@news.supernews.com> <d7i0ji$20c0$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>Perhaps I was unclear.  I believe the consumption of processor time is an
>execution-time resource.  I also see nothing in either SEARCH or SEARCH ALL
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>identity?

I'm not exactly sure what you're asking.  I've always assumed that
processor and elapsed time are very different timings and I would
never expect "identity"?  

By definition SEARCH ALL is a keyed search as opposed to a sequential
search it should be faster in many cases.  The larger the table, the
more difference there should be. Also, of course, SEARCH ALL permits
multiple conditions to be coded in a search.  

>
>The COBOL standard doesn't use "should" on compiler implementors.   The
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I don't know you.  How familiar are you with IBM COBOL
implementations?   If you read the IBM COBOL Language reference, it
defines a format-1 SEARCH as a serial search and format-2 SEARCH ALL
as a binary search. Here's one place you can find that....

http://publib.boulder.ibm.com/infocenter/pdthelp/index.jsp?topic=/com.ibm.entcobol3.doc/rlind.htm


>The expectation that the implementor "should" provide a faster SEARCH ALL
>than is available with an otherwise-identical SEARCH is just that -- an
>expectation that might or might not be met, and it is not an expectation
>that the standard places on the language.  

....and having said that, so what?  We're not talking theory, we're
talking about what's provided in a specific vendors implementation.
If you have something to say that addresses that implementation, I'll
be glad to respond.  



>As some friends of mine are wont
>to advise, "an expectation is a premeditated resentment.".   We need to take
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>standard.

I think you're getting far more generalized that I am.  My responses
were strictly aimed at my experiences with mainframe COBOL. With that
limit your comments are meaningless. 

As a developer or coder I should know my data well enough to make a
practical decision about which version of SEARCH or PERFORM is the
most appropriate.  It has nothing to do with "wanting" or "expecting"
anything.  Know your tools.  


                  
                                          Scott Peterson
```

- **From:** Scott Peterson <scottp4.removethistoreply@mindspring.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<119tbi56lhqk123@news.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<gkf991t55u3mg8n1nrmkvfh8kaogk4s5l6@4ax.com> <d72ga0$94b$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <jpm991922leaap63fquei82jm2lno88c44@4ax.com> <119a8g5hjc729a4@news.supernews.com> <j71b9196v3g2uhuq2tn3r7397bd5n4dib0@4ax.com> <119daflcv5nqt7d@news.supernews.com> <d77er5$1pko$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <119frrqikne09a0@news.supernews.com> <d7i0ji$20c0$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <119t6qu2uhajv46@news.supernews.com> <1117693029.682889.307930@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`

```
"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>
>I think that you have that reversed. SEARCH allows multiple WHEN,
>SEARCH ALL does not.

I guess you'd call it a compound condition instead of multiple.  Sorry
for the lousy formatting but these are cut and pasted from the IBM web
site. 

-SEARCH--identifier-1--+---------------------------+---------->
                         '-VARYING--+-identifier-2-+-'
                                    '-index-name-1-'

--+-------------------------------------+---------------------->
   '-+----+--END--imperative-statement-1-'
     '-AT-'

   .-----------------------------------------------.
   V                                               |
----WHEN--condition-1--+-imperative-statement-2-+-+------------>
                        '-NEXT-SENTENCE----------'

--+------------+----------------------------------------------><
   '-END-SEARCH-'

-SEARCH ALL--identifier-1------------------------------------->

--+-------------------------------------+---------------------->
   '-+----+--END--imperative-statement-1-'
     '-AT-'

--WHEN--+-data-name-1--+----+--+-EQUAL--+----+-+--+-identifier-3------------+-+-->
         |              '-IS-'  |        '-TO-' |
+-literal-1---------------+ |
         |                      '- = -----------'
'-arithmetic-expression-1-' |

'-condition-name-1----------------------------------------------------'


.----------------------------------------------------------------------------------.
   V
|
----+------------------------------------------------------------------------------+-+-->

'-AND--+-data-name-2--+----+--+-EQUAL--+----+-+--+-identifier-4------------+-+-'
            |              '-IS-'  |        '-TO-' |
+-literal-2---------------+ |
            |
'- = -----------' 

'-arithmetic-expression-2-' |

'-condition-name-2----------------------------------------------------'

>--+-imperative-statement-2-+--+------------+------------------><
   '-NEXT SENTENCE----------'  '-END-SEARCH-'


                  
                                          Scott Peterson
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Occurs Depending Memory Use

_(thread depth: 20)_

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-06-02T00:47:17-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1117698437.498928.176910@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<119tbi56lhqk123@news.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<gkf991t55u3mg8n1nrmkvfh8kaogk4s5l6@4ax.com> <d72ga0$94b$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <jpm991922leaap63fquei82jm2lno88c44@4ax.com> <119a8g5hjc729a4@news.supernews.com> <j71b9196v3g2uhuq2tn3r7397bd5n4dib0@4ax.com> <119daflcv5nqt7d@news.supernews.com> <d77er5$1pko$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <119frrqikne09a0@news.supernews.com> <d7i0ji$20c0$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <119t6qu2uhajv46@news.supernews.com> <1117693029.682889.307930@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <119tbi56lhqk123@news.supernews.com>`

```

Scott Peterson wrote:
> "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> site.

In SEARCH each of the multiple WHENs can have compound conditions.

The syntax diagram of the WHEN in a SEARCH ALL is restrictive not
permissive. It indicates that the condition may _only_ be 'equal' and
multiple equals can only be joined by AND.

While each WHEN of a SEARCH specifies a 'condition-1' this can be a
compound conditional of arbitrary complexity. In the WHEN of a SEARCH
ALL it cannot be a 'condition' at all but merely a set of conjoined
equality tests on the fields that make up the key.

No, I would not call the SEARCH ALL a 'compound condition', it is
merely a multi-part equality test. SEARCH _can_ have compound
conditionals, and multiple of them.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Occurs Depending Memory Use

_(thread depth: 18)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2005-06-02T13:45:49+00:00

- **References:** `<d704ka$bth$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <lj6791tjgr1q5n43e8ll3cfr4v7k0l7urf@4ax.com> <d720n3$ku8$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <gkf991t55u3mg8n1nrmkvfh8kaogk4s5l6@4ax.com> <d72ga0$94b$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <jpm991922leaap63fquei82jm2lno88c44@4ax.com> <119a8g5hjc729a4@news.supernews.com> <j71b9196v3g2uhuq2tn3r7397bd5n4dib0@4ax.com> <119daflcv5nqt7d@news.supernews.com> <d77er5$1pko$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <119frrqikne09a0@news.supernews.com> <d7i0ji$20c0$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <119t6qu2uhajv46@news.supernews.com>`

```

On  1-Jun-2005, Scott Peterson <scottp4.removethistoreply@mindspring.com> wrote:

> By definition SEARCH ALL is a keyed search as opposed to a sequential search

What definition are you referring to?   What do you mean by "keyed search"?

All searches use keys.    SEARCH ALL is generally binary, but that is not
mandatory.


> I don't know you.  How familiar are you with IBM COBOL
> implementations?   If you read the IBM COBOL Language reference, it
> defines a format-1 SEARCH as a serial search and format-2 SEARCH ALL
> as a binary search. Here's one place you can find that....

Which is why I don't use SEARCH ALL for small tables on IBM Mainframe searches.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Occurs Depending Memory Use

_(thread depth: 19)_

- **From:** Scott Peterson <scottp4.removethistoreply@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 2005-06-02T09:42:09-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<119udn1cgr4bsb3@news.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<d720n3$ku8$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <gkf991t55u3mg8n1nrmkvfh8kaogk4s5l6@4ax.com> <d72ga0$94b$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <jpm991922leaap63fquei82jm2lno88c44@4ax.com> <119a8g5hjc729a4@news.supernews.com> <j71b9196v3g2uhuq2tn3r7397bd5n4dib0@4ax.com> <119daflcv5nqt7d@news.supernews.com> <d77er5$1pko$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <119frrqikne09a0@news.supernews.com> <d7i0ji$20c0$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <119t6qu2uhajv46@news.supernews.com> <d7n2ic$o33$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>All searches use keys.    SEARCH ALL is generally binary, but that is not
>mandatory.
>

Unless the implementation is documented as using a binary search....as
it is in the case of the IBM compilers we're talking about.  

SEARCH ALL  uses the ASCENDING KEY or DESCENDING KEY option on the
table.  SEARCH does not.  

>
>> I don't know you.  How familiar are you with IBM COBOL
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>Which is why I don't use SEARCH ALL for small tables on IBM Mainframe searches.

Who said you should?  

                  
                                          Scott Peterson

On  2-Jun-2005, Scott Peterson <scottp4.removethistoreply@mindspring.com> wrote:

> >All searches use keys.    SEARCH ALL is generally binary, but that is not
> >mandatory.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> it is in the case of the IBM compilers we're talking about.

I'm talking about CoBOL.   But does this documentation mean that an IBM
optimizer still has to make a SEARCH ALL of the days of the week a binary
search?   (Not that I'd give it that option).

> SEARCH ALL  uses the ASCENDING KEY or DESCENDING KEY option on the
> table.  SEARCH does not.

SEARCH ALL uses sorted keys.    We have to either default a direction or state
the direction.    SEARCH uses unsorted keys, so there is no point in having it
tell us anything about them.
```

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>
>I'm talking about CoBOL. 
>

When I've repeatedly stated I'm only dealing with the IBM
implementation, why do you insist on broadening it and then that the
answer is wrong in the broader sense?  
  

>But does this documentation mean that an IBM optimizer still has to make a 
>SEARCH ALL of the days of the week a binary
>search?   (Not that I'd give it that option).

I don't know what the optimizer has to do with this.  The option about
which search to use is the coders, not the compilers.  Technically if
you had random distribution a sequential search would take 3.5 probes
and a binary search, 3.   Over a couple of million searches you might
save a whole CPU second. <g> 

I don't know why you keep coming back to this though.  I've already
agreed with you that SEARCH ALL is probably not needed for small
tables.  



                  
                                          Scott Peterson

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Occurs Depending Memory Use

_(thread depth: 18)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2005-06-02T10:26:53-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d7nfgt$2736$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<d704ka$bth$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <lj6791tjgr1q5n43e8ll3cfr4v7k0l7urf@4ax.com> <d720n3$ku8$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <gkf991t55u3mg8n1nrmkvfh8kaogk4s5l6@4ax.com> <d72ga0$94b$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <jpm991922leaap63fquei82jm2lno88c44@4ax.com> <119a8g5hjc729a4@news.supernews.com> <j71b9196v3g2uhuq2tn3r7397bd5n4dib0@4ax.com> <119daflcv5nqt7d@news.supernews.com> <d77er5$1pko$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <119frrqikne09a0@news.supernews.com> <d7i0ji$20c0$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <119t6qu2uhajv46@news.supernews.com>`

```

"Scott Peterson" <scottp4.removethistoreply@mindspring.com> wrote in message
news:119t6qu2uhajv46@news.supernews.com...
> "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
>
> >Perhaps I was unclear.  I believe the consumption of processor time is an
> >execution-time resource.  I also see nothing in either SEARCH or SEARCH
ALL
> >that would stand in the way of the constructs being full-bore
> >processor-intensive.  What component of either form of SEARCH would stand
in
> >the way of the correspondence between processor time consumed and elapsed
> >time consumed for either of the two forms being essentially that of
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> never expect "identity"?

If a processor is running full-bore on a given construct, I would expect the
amount of processor time consumed by that construct to be very close to
equal to the *elapsed* time for the construct.  There's nothing except
influences *external* to the construct to interrupt it.

 > By definition SEARCH ALL is a keyed search as opposed to a sequential
> search it should be faster in many cases.

No, not exactly.  Both SEARCH and SEARCH ALL operate on tables that have an
OCCURS ... INDEXED BY.  See below for your comment on "keyed" vs.
"sequential".

> The larger the table, the
> more difference there should be.

Presuming that SEARCH ALL *is* a nonserial (binary or otherwise) search.  It
is *often* true, but not *necessarily* true.

> Also, of course, SEARCH ALL permits
> multiple conditions to be coded in a search.

SEARCH allows *multiple* WHEN clauses, each of which can specify any
conditional expression (simple,  or compound of arbitrary complexity) as
satisfying the search.  SEARCH ALL allows only a *single* WHEN clause; its
conditions must be equality comparisons of keys to the table, and all of
them must be satisfied to satisfy the search.   I'm not sure what
distinction you're trying to make; SEARCH includes all of the qualifiers of
SEARCH ALL as a *subset* of the latter's capabilities; SEARCH ALL is much
more *restricted* in its syntax.

> I don't know you.  How familiar are you with IBM COBOL
> implementations?

It's been a long, long time since I've been directly involved in IBM COBOL;
I have been involved with maintenance and development of Unisys MCP-based
COBOL implementations conforming to the '68, '74 and '85 instantiations of
the corresponding international standards.  Part of that process has
required that I familiarize myself with what the *language standards*
actually say.

> If you read the IBM COBOL Language reference, it
> defines a format-1 SEARCH as a serial search and format-2 SEARCH ALL
> as a binary search. Here's one place you can find that....
>
>
http://publib.boulder.ibm.com/infocenter/pdthelp/index.jsp?topic=/com.ibm.entcobol3.doc/rlind.htm

Goody for them.  Their compiler conforms to the standard.  So does one that
does a serial search for both.

As to what COBOL *in general* is supposed, or required, to do, as distinct
from what IBM thinks it ought to do for their customers, I stand on the
distinction in the wording in ANSI X3.23-1985 between page VI-123, General
Rule 2:  "If format 1 of the SEARCH statement is used, a serial type of
search operation takes place ..." and page VI-124, General Rule 4:  "If
Format 2 of the SEARCH is used, a nonserial type of search operation may
take place ..." ('74 and '02 standards have similar wording).  The
distinction between "takes place" in the first case and "may take place" in
the second may seem subtle, but ain't.  An implementation that does a serial
search for SEARCH ALL conforms to the standard for SEARCH ALL so long as it
retrieves the correct datum; so does one that does a binary search.  Our
COBOL85 implementation does a binary search too.  That doesn't mean that
SEARCH ALL is *defined as* producing a binary search.   The syntax is
specifically chosen so that an implementor is not *precluded* from doing a
binary search.

> ....and having said that, so what?  We're not talking theory, we're
> talking about what's provided in a specific vendors implementation.
> If you have something to say that addresses that implementation, I'll
> be glad to respond.

As an active participant in both INCITS/J4 and ISO/IEC JTC1/SC22/WG4, in
terms of what I expect COBOL to do, what individual implementations do is of
far less importance to me than what users should expect of standard COBOL
*in general*.   That goes for Unisys MCP COBOL implementations, with which
I'm fairly intimately familiar, just as much as it does for IBM COBOL
implementations, to which my last significant exposure was some thirty-five
years ago.   .

The *COBOL* verb SEARCH ALL is *not* required by any COBOL *standard* to do
a binary search (or for that matter a combination of a binary and a serial
search).  The authors of the standard have carefully worded the requirements
so that this is the case; in fact, the standard doesn't say anything at all
about what sort of search algorithm should be used; it addresses *results*,
not *methods*.  I can also observe from experience that the authors of the
COBOL standard are also more concerned about *accuracy* than *performance*,
and the distinction between SEARCH and SEARCH ALL for data and search
criteria that meet the more stringent requirements of the latter is
*strictly* one of performance.  Whether the improved performance of a
strictly-binary SEARCH ALL is worth the extra performance is a matter for
discussion betwen the *implementor* and the *implementor's customers*.

> I think you're getting far more generalized that I am.  My responses
> were strictly aimed at my experiences with mainframe COBOL. With that
> limit your comments are meaningless.

Yes, I am.  From several standpoints that's appropriate.  I've been
participating on the US and international COBOL standards committees, and as
such I believe it is appropriate for me to take a broader view than the
particular opinions of one vendor (including my employer!).

And I've been maintaining "mainframe COBOL" compilers since September 1984.
There are architectures that qualify as "mainframes" other than those with
which you are familiar.

> As a developer or coder I should know my data well enough to make a
> practical decision about which version of SEARCH or PERFORM is the
> most appropriate.  It has nothing to do with "wanting" or "expecting"
> anything.  Know your tools.

I agree wholeheartedly.  But that also implies that it's inappropriate to
generalize your experiences with a particular implementation of COBOL to
come to the conclusion that those experiences are applicable to COBOL in
general.  *Your* tools may not be *the only* tools, or even *the only
appropriate* tools, and it's important also to know the difference.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>If a processor is running full-bore on a given construct, I would expect the
>amount of processor time consumed by that construct to be very close to
>equal to the *elapsed* time for the construct.  There's nothing except
>influences *external* to the construct to interrupt it.

The way that IBM handles job accounting, CPU will almost always be
fare less than elapsed time.  As I remember, UNIX reports it
differently as you don't really have job steps in the sense that IBM
big iron does  so it's an apple/oranges comparison.  

>
> > By definition SEARCH ALL is a keyed search as opposed to a sequential
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>"sequential".

But SEARCH ALL requires the INDEXED option, SEARCH does not.  

>> The larger the table, the
>> more difference there should be.
>
>Presuming that SEARCH ALL *is* a nonserial (binary or otherwise) search.  It
>is *often* true, but not *necessarily* true.

So what's the problem?  I said from the first exchange that this
assumes fairly random distribution and even provided an example where
you it would be better not to use SEARCH ALL. But if you can use it,
the differences should be substantial on large tables.  

>
>> I don't know you.  How familiar are you with IBM COBOL
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

>does a serial search for both.

>distinction in the wording in ANSI X3.23-1985 between page VI-123,

>
>As an active participant in both INCITS/J4 and ISO/IEC JTC1/SC22/WG4, in
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

>The *COBOL* verb SEARCH ALL is *not* required by any COBOL *standard* to do
>a binary search (or for that matter a combination of a binary and a serial
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

>I can also observe from experience that the authors of the

…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
'

>> I think you're getting far more generalized that I am.  My responses

…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>particular opinions of one vendor (including my employer!).

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Occurs Depending Memory Use

_(thread depth: 20)_

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>

- **References:** `<d704ka$bth$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <lj6791tjgr1q5n43e8ll3cfr4v7k0l7urf@4ax.com> <d720n3$ku8$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <gkf991t55u3mg8n1nrmkvfh8kaogk4s5l6@4ax.com> <d72ga0$94b$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <jpm991922leaap63fquei82jm2lno88c44@4ax.com> <119a8g5hjc729a4@news.supernews.com> <j71b9196v3g2uhuq2tn3r7397bd5n4dib0@4ax.com> <119daflcv5nqt7d@news.supernews.com> <d77er5$1pko$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <119frrqikne09a0@news.supernews.com> <d7i0ji$20c0$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <119t6qu2uhajv46@news.supernews.com>`

```
"Scott Peterson" <scottp4.removethistoreply@mindspring.com> wrote in message 
news:119t6qu2uhajv46@news.supernews.com...
> "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
>
<snip>
>
> By definition SEARCH ALL is a keyed search as opposed to a sequential
> search it should be faster in many cases.
<snip>

Scott,
   This is the point that people keep missing.  A SEARCH ALL is
           *permitted*
to be a "keyed search as opposed to a sequential search".  There is NOTHING in 
the ANSI/ISO Standard that requires this.  The Standard *does* require that the 
table be in "keyed" order - but there is nothing to say that the compiler "use" 
this information.  Most (all?) of the CURRENT "production-level" compilers that 
I know of, do use a "keyed" (binary) search - but I (like Chuck) know of some 
that have not always done so.  He may know of some that don't even today.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d7nks1$2a6b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<d704ka$bth$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <lj6791tjgr1q5n43e8ll3cfr4v7k0l7urf@4ax.com> <d720n3$ku8$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <gkf991t55u3mg8n1nrmkvfh8kaogk4s5l6@4ax.com> <d72ga0$94b$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <jpm991922leaap63fquei82jm2lno88c44@4ax.com> <119a8g5hjc729a4@news.supernews.com> <j71b9196v3g2uhuq2tn3r7397bd5n4dib0@4ax.com> <119daflcv5nqt7d@news.supernews.com> <d77er5$1pko$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <119frrqikne09a0@news.supernews.com> <d7i0ji$20c0$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <119t6qu2uhajv46@news.supernews.com> <17Ine.142723$uu5.68357@fe05.news.easynews.com>`

```

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:17Ine.142723$uu5.68357@fe05.news.easynews.com...
> "Scott Peterson" <scottp4.removethistoreply@mindspring.com> wrote in
message
> news:119t6qu2uhajv46@news.supernews.com...
> > "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> to be a "keyed search as opposed to a sequential search".  There is
NOTHING in
> the ANSI/ISO Standard that requires this.  The Standard *does* require
that the
> table be in "keyed" order - but there is nothing to say that the compiler
"use"
> this information.  Most (all?) of the CURRENT "production-level" compilers
that
> I know of, do use a "keyed" (binary) search - but I (like Chuck) know of
some
> that have not always done so.  He may know of some that don't even today.

Unisys MCP COBOL74, which is not only still in use but remains a
fully-supported product, uses a serial search for SEARCH ALL.

On the other hand, Unisys MCP COBOL85 uses a binary search for SEARCH ALL.

Note that the results are compliant with the standard either way.  We are
striving to convince our existing COBOL74 users (a substantial percentage of
the Unisys MCP user base, truth be told!) to migrate to COBOL85 using a
number of incentives.  Among those is that we are not accepting
customer-submitted New Feature Suggestions against the COBOL74 compiler
(although we continue to enhance the compiler to account for new Unisys
hardware and software developments).

Since we're not accepting customer suggestions, and enhancements are pretty
much limited to what's required to keep the compiler doing what it's doing
in current and future environments, we are not planning to revise the code
generated for SEARCH ALL by the COBOL74 compiler so as to improve the
execution-time performance of its generated code.

To those customers who would request that we make changes to the COBOL74
compiler to improve the performance of the generated obuject code (for
SEARCH ALL and elsewhere), our basic response is that those users who want
the improved performance associated with the code generated by the COBOL85
compiler for this construct (and others) are best served by using a compiler
that generates this improved code, namely, the COBOL85 compiler, in
preference to one that does not.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Occurs Depending Memory Use

_(thread depth: 19)_

- **From:** Scott Peterson <scottp4.removethistoreply@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 2005-06-02T14:24:50-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<119uu91f01jma39@news.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<d720n3$ku8$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <gkf991t55u3mg8n1nrmkvfh8kaogk4s5l6@4ax.com> <d72ga0$94b$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <jpm991922leaap63fquei82jm2lno88c44@4ax.com> <119a8g5hjc729a4@news.supernews.com> <j71b9196v3g2uhuq2tn3r7397bd5n4dib0@4ax.com> <119daflcv5nqt7d@news.supernews.com> <d77er5$1pko$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <119frrqikne09a0@news.supernews.com> <d7i0ji$20c0$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <119t6qu2uhajv46@news.supernews.com> <17Ine.142723$uu5.68357@fe05.news.easynews.com>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

> This is the point that people keep missing.  A SEARCH ALL is
>           *permitted*
>to be a "keyed search as opposed to a sequential search".  There is NOTHING in 
>the ANSI/ISO Standard that requires this. 

I fully agree with you, Bill.  As was pointed out, all the standard
really says is that with SEARCH ALL a non-linear search is permitted.

All I've tried to point out is that the IBM mainframe compilers do
implement this using a binary search.  
                  
                                          Scott Peterson

Hi, I'm roby...
I was studying cobol language in order to port a vb application to cobol
application using cobol-85.
First of all I wanted to test some code about cobol array management.
I'm trying to porting vb array management (redim preserve) to cobol
I know using of occurs time and depending on instruction
I wrote this code and it seems doesn't store data correctly
into array when index exceeded size
I thought I only need to change INDEX-VALUE to change array dimension

thanks for help


       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID. STR01.
       AUTHOR. ROBY.
           INSTALLATION. ROME.
           DATE-WRITTEN. 30/05/2005.
           DATE-COMPILED. 31/05/2005.
           SECURITY. NONE.

       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       CONFIGURATION SECTION.
       SOURCE-COMPUTER. AMD-SEMPRON.
       OBJECT-COMPUTER. AMD-SEMPRON.
      *SPECIAL-NAMES. DECIMAL-POINT IS COMMA.
       SPECIAL-NAMES. CURRENCY SIGN IS "E".

       DATA DIVISION.
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       77 POS PIC 99.
       77 INDEX-VALUE PIC 99.
       77 CT PIC 99 VALUE 0.

       01 INPUT-STRING PIC X(20).
       01 KEY-PRESSED PIC X.

       01 TABLE-STRING.
	  02 TAB01 OCCURS 1 TO 3 TIMES DEPENDING ON INDEX-VALUE.
	     05 NAME PIC X(20) VALUE SPACES.

       01 PRINT-LINE.
	  02 FILLER PIC X(10)  VALUE "RECORD #".
	  02 OUT-NUMBER PIC 99.
	  02 FILLER PIC X(3)   VALUE SPACES.
	  02 OUT-NAME PIC X(20).


       PROCEDURE DIVISION.

	   MOVE 1 TO INDEX-VALUE.

       START-INPUT.
           DISPLAY "INSERT YOUR DATA (MAX 50)"
	   ACCEPT INPUT-STRING FROM CONSOLE
	   DISPLAY "INPUT = '" INPUT-STRING "'"

	   IF INPUT-STRING = " " GO TO END-OF-PROGRAM.
	   PERFORM SAVE-DATA
	   GO TO START-INPUT.

       PRINT-DATA.
	   MOVE 1 TO CT
      	   PERFORM UNTIL CT = INDEX-VALUE
               MOVE CT TO OUT-NUMBER
               MOVE NAME(CT) TO OUT-NAME
               DISPLAY PRINT-LINE
	       ADD 1 TO CT
           END-PERFORM.
           DISPLAY "END-PERFORM".
       PRINT-DATA-EX.
 	   EXIT.

       SAVE-DATA.
	   MOVE INPUT-STRING TO NAME(INDEX-VALUE)
	   DISPLAY "DATA STORE AT INDEX " INDEX-VALUE
	   ADD 1 TO INDEX-VALUE.
       SAVE-DATA-EX.
	   EXIT.

       END-OF-PROGRAM.
	   PERFORM PRINT-DATA
           DISPLAY "PRESS ANY KEY"
           ACCEPT KEY-PRESSED FROM CONSOLE
	   STOP RUN.

#### ↳ Re: question about dynamic array

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2005-06-01T13:29:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<kLine.9276$iA6.2752@newssvr19.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<d7kco0$pl$1@newsreader.mailgate.org>`

```
"Roby66" <NOSPAM@libero.it> wrote in message
news:d7kco0$pl$1@newsreader.mailgate.org...
> I thought I only need to change INDEX-VALUE to change array dimension

Changing INDEX-VALUE will change the maximum subscript you may access, but
the table (that's what BASIC arrays are usually called by COBOL programmers)
may never have more than 'max' elements as specified in the "OCCURS  min to
max"  clause; e.g.,

>   02 TAB01 OCCURS 1 TO 3 TIMES DEPENDING ON INDEX-VALUE.

You may never have more than three valid elements in this table.

MCM
```

```

>
>You may never have more than three valid elements in this table.
>

Is there another way to use dynamic array in cobol ?
Or this force to me to declare statically an array ?

Roby
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: question about dynamic array

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2005-06-01T13:56:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<j8jne.9438$iA6.9337@newssvr19.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<d7kco0$pl$1@newsreader.mailgate.org> <kLine.9276$iA6.2752@newssvr19.news.prodigy.com> <d7kdvu$2pg$1@newsreader.mailgate.org>`

```
"Roby66" <NOSPAM@libero.it> wrote in message
news:d7kdvu$2pg$1@newsreader.mailgate.org...
>
> >
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Or this force to me to declare statically an array ?

Not in the sense you, as a BASIC-language programmer, would understand
'dynamic array'

The only way to do this in COBOL is to use a COBOL compiler which supports
calls to the current operating system to do your own memory
allocation/reallocation/deallocation and then use POINTER data types to
access that data.

Yes, you could find some programmer to create the code needed, and structure
it to resemble BASIC. But any such code will perforce be limited to a
specific compiler on a specific target operating system.

Or, instead of using a 'memory' table/array, you could use a random-access
file; see ORGANIZATION SEQUENTIAL ACCESS RANDOM RELATIVE KEY IS
relative-key-name in your SELECT statements.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: question about dynamic array

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2005-06-01T17:48:05+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<pxmne.119170$Yr4.75689@fe07.news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<d7kco0$pl$1@newsreader.mailgate.org> <kLine.9276$iA6.2752@newssvr19.news.prodigy.com> <d7kdvu$2pg$1@newsreader.mailgate.org> <j8jne.9438$iA6.9337@newssvr19.news.prodigy.com> <d7kg5b$2pg$2@newsreader.mailgate.org>`

```
If you are talking about "VS COBOL II" on an IBM mainframe, you should know that 
compiler is out-of-service and has been for many years.  The currently supported 
IBM compiler (that does include the '85 Standard *PLUS* intrinsic functions 
*PLUS* Object orientation PLUS lots of extensions) is "Enterprise COBOL". You 
will need to find out exactly which compiler is your target - to know what you 
can and cannot code.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: question about dynamic array

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2005-06-01T08:14:58-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d7kjdi$gd4$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<d7kco0$pl$1@newsreader.mailgate.org> <kLine.9276$iA6.2752@newssvr19.news.prodigy.com> <d7kdvu$2pg$1@newsreader.mailgate.org> <j8jne.9438$iA6.9337@newssvr19.news.prodigy.com>`

```
"Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote in message
news:j8jne.9438$iA6.9337@newssvr19.news.prodigy.com...

> Or, instead of using a 'memory' table/array, you could use a random-access
> file; see ORGANIZATION SEQUENTIAL ACCESS RANDOM RELATIVE KEY IS
> relative-key-name in your SELECT statements.

Not in *standard* COBOL (though this, or close variations of this, is a
common extension).

The only ACCESS MODE allowed for ORGANIZATION SEQUENTIAL files in standard
'74, '85 or '02 COBOL is SEQUENTIAL.  See ANSI X3.23-1974 page IV-4, ANSI
X3.23-1985 page VII-7, and ISO/IEC 1989:2002 page 212 for the applicable
syntax diagrams.

However, you could substitute ORGANIZATION RELATIVE for ORGANIZATION
SEQUENTIAL into the above syntax and come up with standard COBOL.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

#### ↳ Re: question about dynamic array

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2005-06-01T18:03:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<yLmne.11002$iA6.3818@newssvr19.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<d7kco0$pl$1@newsreader.mailgate.org>`

```
Just noticed...

"Roby66" <NOSPAM@libero.it> wrote in message
news:d7kco0$pl$1@newsreader.mailgate.org...
> Hi, I'm roby...
> I'm trying to porting vb array management (redim preserve) to cobol
...
[anither message.]..
>Anyway I have to port this vb application on mainframe..
...
>I only know new application has to be developed
>using cobol II, I think cobol 85.
>Customer sent me a source code is running on
>their system and I suppose is standard ansi85

???

Not sure I understand your situation entirely, but let me hazard a guess:

Customer sent you COBOL source code of ..what, what they use now?  And you
need to integrate some capability currently available in a VB program into
that software?

Not ever having seen any of this, or even sure if I understand the situation
correctly, two things come to mind...

1. There is such a thing as "Mainframe BASIC".  IIRC, it's not terribly
popular or powerful, but it's out there somewhere.
2. Instead of screwing around with a port, might be easier and surely will
be more maintainable long-term to forget about 'porting,'  bite the bullet
and just write the required logic in COBOL from scratch. (Disclaimer: I am a
notorious critic of 'verb-for-verb' port/translation efforts).

FWIW, if your BASIC is well written, your COBOL code - if equally
well-written - will look a lot like the BASIC code with which you
start.(I've often said "Well-written BASIC looks a lot like well-written
COBOL. Badly-written BASIC looks a lot like, well, badly-written  BASIC".

(Also FWIW: dynamic memory allocation and pointer variables ARE available
with IBM mainrame COBOL).

MCM
```

## Thread: [OT] IBM Mainframe Storage - DVD Drives? — 9 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### [OT] IBM Mainframe Storage - DVD Drives?

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2005-06-01T10:56:15-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d7kiaf$n56$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```

So here's the situation... case upon case of 9-track tape reels, taking up 
space and incurring storage costs.

Someone suggests transferring them to DVD... is there such a thing as a 
mainframe DVD-jukebox?  If so, where might such a thing be researched?  A 
Google search on "mainframe DVD drive" yields zero hits while '"DVD drive" 
mainframe' yields over 8,000 pages.

DD
```

#### ↳ Re: IBM Mainframe Storage - DVD Drives?

- **From:** epc8@juno.com
- **Date:** 2005-06-01T19:03:47-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1117677826.890937.42620@g47g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<d7kiaf$n56$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```


docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
> So here's the situation... case upon case of 9-track tape reels, taking up
> space and incurring storage costs.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> DD

A quick Google search on "dvd jukebox" and mainframe gives a smaller
number of hits. Apparently JVC and Pioneer make them.

But will there be enough storage capacity? There's a large JVC 600 disk
unit (double sided DVD) that stores 5.64 TB. At 50 GB per mag tape
that's about 112 tapes.

It looks like they use SCSI.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IBM Mainframe Storage - DVD Drives?

- **From:** "Joel C. Ewing" <jcREMOVEewing@CAPSacm.org>
- **Date:** 2005-06-02T16:10:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<gcGne.5164$MI4.719@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<d7mvoc$9v2$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<d7kiaf$n56$1@panix5.panix.com> <1117677826.890937.42620@g47g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <d7mvoc$9v2$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
> In article <1117677826.890937.42620@g47g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>,
>  <epc8@juno.com> wrote:
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>>that's about 112 tapes.

Max capacity of 2400' 9-track tape reel at 6250 bpi with an efficient
blocksize is closer to 120 MB, not 50 GB.  That would potentially allow
5+ tape images even on an ordinary CD, or around 39+ on a single 4.7GB
DVD.  It is very unlikely that 100% of the tape capacity was used in
most cases, so the actual number of tapes per DVD could be much higher.

While IBM does have an optical solution using a 3995 device and OAM, you
wouldn't be able to access the data in the same way as a tape dataset.
A solution that involves multiple optical media, 3995 or some DVD
solution, would reduce the size of the media storage, but if you
actually need to read the data you would be trading the mechanical
problems of 9-track drives for the mechanical problems of a "jukebox",
which can be significant.

One interesting thought, which I would be tempted to explore as a
possibility:  a mere 400 GB of hard drive space on on workstation (not
that expensive these days) could potentially store over 30,000 9-track
tapes.  If that workstation has TCP/IP connectivity to the system
needing to access the 9-track tapes, then it could run as an NFS file
server, store tape images, and make images of the tapes remotely
available to an NFS client on the system needing the data.  An ordinary
DVD writer on the workstation could be used for backups and Disaster
Recovery of the images on the workstation, but you wouldn't be exposed
to media handling and the associated mechanical failures during normal
reading of the data from the hard drive.  This should be cheap to
experiment with if you already have IP connectivity and an idle
workstation capable of running Linux; and the data transport to the
target system doesn't require any expensive interfaces or the invention
of any new techniques.


>>It looks like they use SCSI.
> 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

There's no question that it can be done... but whenever a solution, 
especially one that requires new hardware being bought and installed, is 
suggested two questions are hurled nigh simultaneously:

'How much will it cost?'

'How much work will it take?'

... and no matter what the numbers are the replies then come:

'Costs too much!'

'Too much work... my people are already swamped.'

But... the researching is proving interesting, at least.

Thanks much!

DD
