###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: interesting use of NEXT SENTENCE vs. CONTINUE

_(thread depth: 24)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2005-06-10T13:32:08-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d8ctcg$q7j$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<3g8q52Fb2itjU1@individual.net> <1117863336.941688.184770@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <fvboe.185329$uu5.62495@fe05.news.easynews.com> <1117874000.565517.100420@g44g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <d7s7o9$6ol$1@panix5.panix.com> <3gfukvFc4h3qU1@individual.net> <d81md9$pmu$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3gk48iFcotqsU1@individual.net> <d84fb0$1qbd$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3gmlfjFd8f0qU1@individual.net> <d859v3$2a99$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3gnta2Fd8eb8U1@individual.net> <d87edh$ide$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3gpibiFdiejoU1@individual.net> <d89the$223l$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3gsc9uFe0rs5U1@individual.net>`

```

"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3gsc9uFe0rs5U1@individual.net...

> This is where I argue that the TIME to do that compare cannot be faster
than
> the time to do your branch (irrespective of the branch being unconditional
> or conditional, or static, or dynamic.)

...

> If your 'comparison' breaks down into a series of steps (and you are not
the
> only architure where this happens) then we need to get the cycle time for
> each of the steps.

I found some figures from a somewhat-recent system.

First case:  Presuming no concatenation, branch prediction and so forth, it
appears that BRUN consumes one clock or less.

Second case:  The collection of operations (say, four instructions -- for
example VALC, ZERO, EQUL, BRTR) required to retrieve properly-described
numeric values to the stack, compare them against numeric literals, and
branch conditionally based on that comparison should take four clocks or
less.

In contrast, depending on the architecture, the three instructions needed to
accomplish a dynamic branch from an empty-stack state consume from around
fifteen to as many as seventy clock cycles.  Two of these are one clock or
less (NAMC, LODT); it's the dynamic branch itself -- DBUN, as I've said
before -- that's the killer.  When ya gotta use it, ya gotta use it.

> The whole of my contention is that comparison MUST take longer than
> branching. Nothing to do with ALTER (any more...:-))
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> architecture, but logic and experience tell me it simply doesn't make
sense.
> So there must be something I am missing here... or not.)

DBUN is now, and has always been, more costly than the simplest of numeric
comparisons taken in combination with static conditional branches, to say
nothing of being more costly than the comparisons taken without associated
logic to test the conditions.

To return to the original point:  It is *not* a universal truism that ALTER
performs better than using an explicit run-time switch.

There are environments in which testing an explicit run-time switch in the
source code is significantly more efficient than using ALTER.

And to answer the more recent point:  there are environments in which the
testing of a simple run-time switch does not outweigh the cost of *some*
unconditional branches.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

believe

…[6 more quoted lines elided]…

And BRUN takes one clock cycle. So what does DBUN do and why would it be

I NEVER said it did and this is gross distortion of what I DID say. My
statement was qualified to specific platforms in cases where memory was in
short supply. I would EXPECT it to be an "universal truism" and am genuinely
interested as to why it apparently isn't.

Here's what I said:

"To digress for a moment, in Odene Dayse I worked on sites where ALTER was
used  as a matter of course. It was extremely efficient and, in machines
with limited memory, was MUCH better than using IF for a first time switch,
for example. With IF, the first time condition is tested for every iteration
of the code; with ALTER, once only...(and IF required other machine code
overheads, which ALTER didn't.)".

There is no mention of 'universal truisms' there. It is, like all the
statements I make in posts here, limited to my personal experience. My
comment is qualified to "sites I worked on where ALTER was used as a matter
of course, and memory was in short supply".

> source code is significantly more efficient than using ALTER.
>

> testing of a simple run-time switch does not outweigh the cost of *some*
> unconditional branches.
>

Not proven.

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: interesting use of NEXT SENTENCE vs. CONTINUE

_(thread depth: 26)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2005-06-20T12:24:09-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d9754g$1gt3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<3g8q52Fb2itjU1@individual.net> <1117863336.941688.184770@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <fvboe.185329$uu5.62495@fe05.news.easynews.com> <1117874000.565517.100420@g44g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <d7s7o9$6ol$1@panix5.panix.com> <3gfukvFc4h3qU1@individual.net> <d81md9$pmu$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3gk48iFcotqsU1@individual.net> <d84fb0$1qbd$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3gmlfjFd8f0qU1@individual.net> <d859v3$2a99$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3gnta2Fd8eb8U1@individual.net> <d87edh$ide$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3gpibiFdiejoU1@individual.net> <d89the$223l$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3gsc9uFe0rs5U1@individual.net> <d8ctcg$q7j$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3h19rgFeqgf2U1@individual.net>`

```

"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3h19rgFeqgf2U1@individual.net...
> This is a good post. Thanks.

> > I found some figures from a somewhat-recent system.
> >
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> manuals so could you please translate this op code into English? Is this
an
> unconditional Branch?

Yes.  BRanch UNconditional.

> >
> > Second case:  The collection of operations (say, four instructions -- 
for
> > example VALC, ZERO, EQUL, BRTR) required to retrieve properly-described
> > numeric values to the stack, compare them against numeric literals, and
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> tranlsate this into English (at least once).

I'll start off with some other terms used a lot below.

TOS = Top-of-Stack.  Call it a register if you like.  Whether it is actually
a hardware register or not depends on the particular system model.  Heck,
for that matter, whether the Unisys MCP system as a whole is "hardware" or
not depends on the particular system model, so distinctions about the
specifics of "hardware" are largely moot.

PUSH = a machine instruction that ensures that the TOS value is actually
represented in the stack itself on systems that have a TOS register separate
from the stack.  In the context of the discussion below, "pushing" something
onto the top of the stack is intended to denote the action of adding
something to the stack rather than replacing the TOS value.

Another important point about Unisys MCP system is that every word in the
system's memory has a "tag" field associated with it.  Even-numbered tags
represent operands; all we need to worry about here are single-precision
oeprands for which the tag field is zero.  Words with odd-numbered tags are
protected from "accidental" access as data.  Words with tags of 3 (untouched
Code Segment Descriptor), 5 (untouched Data Descriptor) and 7 (Program
Control Word) might be referenced in the discussion below.

Back to the simplest form of "If <dataname> IS EQUAL TO ZERO GO TO
<some-paragraph>" and the equivalent machine instructions.  The whole list:
VALueCall, put a ZERO on the stack, compare for EQUaLity (sets low-order bit
of TopTOS word t, staticBranchifTRue.

Just for grins, let's assume the evaluation stack is EMPTY when we start
this sequence.

VALC retrieves a value from the stack at the stack cell location specified
in the instruction and pushes it onto TOS; stack now contains one entry.

ZERO, as implied, pushes an all-bits-off, tag-zero (single-precision
operand) word onto TOS.   Stack now contains two entries.

EQUL compares the two operands, consumes them from the stack, and pushes the
truth value of the result (0 = false, unequal; 1 = true, equal) as an
operand onto TOS.  Stack now contains only this boolean result.

BRTR includes the address of the instruction *within the current code
segment* (if the instruction weren't in the current code segment we'd have
to use NAMC, LODT, DBUN -- see below).  If the low-order bit of TOS is set,
the branch happens; if the low-order bit is reset, it doesn't.  Whether the
branch is taken or not, the evaluation stack is EMPTY again.

Note that none of these instructions reference anything outside the stack
(code, data, system tables, anything); everything they do is "local" within
the stack -- the retrieval of the value, the comparison against zero, and
the branch conditional on the result of that comparison.

Now on to the other case.

 > In contrast, depending on the architecture, the three instructions needed
> to
> > accomplish a dynamic branch from an empty-stack state consume from
around
> > fifteen to as many as seventy clock cycles.  Two of these are one clock
or
> > less (NAMC, LODT); it's the dynamic branch itself -- DBUN, as I've said
> > before -- that's the killer.  When ya gotta use it, ya gotta use it.

NAMeCall, LOaDTransparent, DynamicBranchUNconditional.

Here's what happens with NAMC, LODT, DBUN:

The Name Call turns the stack location in the instruction into an Indirect
Reference Word so that what it points to can be further manipulated, and
puts that IRW on top of the stack.

Load Transparent takes the value to which the IRW on the top of the stack
points -- whatever it is that it points to -- and loads that word onto the
top of the stack, *complete with the tag bits*.  In this particular case,
what it points to could be a Stuffed Indirect Reference Word (Tag 1) -- or a
chain of them -- pointing to a Program Control Word (Tag 7), or it could
itself contain a PCW (which is usually the case here).  The PCW includes
(among other things) the word and byte-offset address of the machine
instruction to which it points, but more importantly the number of the entry
in the Actual Segment Descriptor table that is managed by the system and
controls the allocation of memory.  The ASD table is used to control the
presence/absence of information in memory.

A Dynamic branch (conditional or otherwise) has two forms.  The first is a
relative branch, forward or backward in halfword -- three code syllable -- 
increments within the current code segment.  This is what happens when the
tag bits on the Top of Stack value indicate an *operand*, and doesn't
concern us here.   The second occurs when the Top of Stack contains either a
PCW, or an Indirect Reference Word that points to a PCW; the contents of
that PCW (simplistically put) point to an instruction address somewhere in
the object code file, encoded as code segment number, the offset in words
from the beginning of that code segment, and the syllable within the word.
Note that this information is the same whether the object code segment is in
memory or not, or even whether it is in the same location as it was the last
time the object code instruction being pointed  to was executed or not.  The
"reference chain" from the PCW goes through the program's Lex Level 1 stack
of code segment descriptors to the system's ASD table managed by both the
MCP and the hardware, to get to the ultimate code address, and it's outside
the purview of the program (or the user) where that code segment resides or
whether or not it needs to be loaded into memory.

> And BRUN takes one clock cycle.

BRUN might even take *less* than a clock cycle, depending on the particular
implementation.

> So what does DBUN do and why would it be
> used in preference to BRUN? "Translate" these instruction sequences into
> what they do, in English, a language we both share, unless you have some
> reason not to...I have not thrown a heap of IBM Assembler, (or any other)
Op
> Codes at you, to support my position; can you not show me the same
courtesy?

There are times where DBUN *must* be used.  The main one is for a branch
(conditonal or otherwise) to a location in a different object code segment
in the program.   Given a relatively small intra-segment code addressing
capability (8K words, 6 syllables per word), despite a reasonably-powerful
and economical instruction set producing a relatively dense
code-to-statement ratio, it may prove efficacious for programmers to be
aware as to whether a "segment break" occurs in the middle of a critical
path in the program.

On Unisys MCP systems, an unconditional branch to an instruction in a
different code segment will almost certainly be *much* slower than a simple
numeric comparison; indeed, it will be *much* slower than a simple numeric
test in combination with an associated conditional branch.  It does *not
matter* whether the Unisys MCP system has limited memory or not (although
the difference would be quite a bit *more* on a limited-memory machine
precisely because of the higher likelihood that the code segment to which
the PCW pointed would have been overwritten by data, requiring the MCP to
handle a PresenceBit interrupt to find a suitable place somewhere in memory
into which to read the object code segment, followed by the actual I/O to
accomplish that).    Even on systems with sufficient memory, an
unconditional cross-segment branch will probably be *at least* an order of
magnitude slower than a numeric comparison involving one or more USAGE
BINARY data items.  It takes a fair amount of "conditional expression" code
to consume as much processor as a cross-segment branch, conditional or
otherwise.

> I NEVER said it did and this is gross distortion of what I DID say. My
> statement was qualified to specific platforms in cases where memory was in
> short supply. I would EXPECT it to be an "universal truism" and am
genuinely
> interested as to why it apparently isn't.

As I read it, the determining factor was the amount of memory on the system,
not the nature of the particular platform on which that limited memory
occurred.

And the premise underlying your conclusion seemed to be a second "universal
truism":  a comparison (whether followed by a conditional branch or not)
will *always* be slower than an unconditional branch.

I have attempted to demonstrate that that "universal truism" ain't
universally true.

> Here's what I said:
>
> "To digress for a moment, in Odene Dayse I worked on sites where ALTER was
> used  as a matter of course. It was extremely efficient and, in machines
> with limited memory, was MUCH better than using IF for a first time
switch,
> for example. With IF, the first time condition is tested for every
iteration
> of the code; with ALTER, once only...(and IF required other machine code
> overheads, which ALTER didn't.)".

Yes, I understand.  I was responding to this paragraph.

You don't mention *platforms*, you mention *sites*.  I could postulate that
those sites were limited to a small subset of the available architectures,
but that's not stated.

ALTER was not then, and is not now, extremely efficient on all machines
regardless of memory capacity. You had experience in sites in which that
might have been the case, but neither that experience nor that conclusion is
universally applicable to all sites or to all architectures.

ALTER was not then, and is not now, "in machines with limited memory, ...
MUCH better than using IF for a first-time switch.".   You had experience in
sites in which that that might have been the case, but neither that
experience nor that conclusion is universally applicable to all sites or to
all architectures.

I agree that such statements might be true *for systems of a particular
architecture*.   Above, you state "I would EXPECT it to be an "universal
truism" and am genuinely interested as to why it apparently isn't."   The
answer is that neither all comparisons nor all control-transfer instructions
industry-wide are independent of the architecture of the platform.
Comparisons can be fast or slow.  Control-transfer instructions can be fast
or slow.  It is *possible* for an unconditional branch to consume many times
the processor resources that a simple comparison would consume.   I've given
the examples above.

>
> There is no mention of 'universal truisms' there. It is, like all the
> statements I make in posts here, limited to my personal experience. My
> comment is qualified to "sites I worked on where ALTER was used as a
matter
> of course, and memory was in short supply".
>
> > There are environments in which testing an explicit run-time switch in
the
> > source code is significantly more efficient than using ALTER.
> >
>
> And the results of a test designed to explore that have not been posted.

I've been busy doing other things.  I've given you the principles, and my
experiments support my conclusions.   I've gone to the trouble to describe
in great detail *why* those experiments support my conclusions.

> > And to answer the more recent point:  there are environments in which
the
> > testing of a simple run-time switch does not outweigh the cost of *some*
> > unconditional branches.

> Not proven.

I think it's been amply demonstrated.  Get yourself a ClearPathPlus LX140
Software Developer's Kit with the appropriate compilers and you can see for
yourself!   I'm reluctant to post specific numbers because the numbers would
vary widely depending on the particular platform within the ClearPathPlus
Libra and LX series systems on which the tests were being run.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
On Tue, 7 Jun 2005 16:17:42 -0700, "Chuck Stevens"
<charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>> much snipped
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
When I went from programming an RCA301 in COBOL to an IBM 360 using
DOS COBOL, I was faced with memory limitations (a 16K partition).
Being much younger, I looked at the code generated for PERFORM.  The
RCA 301 COBOL used V1 Address-to-store-Return-Address,Jump-to-Address.
The 360 generated three moves and a branch.  An ALTER was just a MOVE.
So where I would have coded PERFORM on the 301, I coded ALTER followed
by GO TO.  The saving grace in these programs was all of the ALTERed
GO TOs were SWnnnn-semi-meaningful-name paragraphs.  All paragraphs in
the program were sequentially numbered.  I would not core scrounge in
this manner if I had the same problem today.  Some of the programs
coded with this philosophy lasted into the 1990's (Ugh) even though
from a business point of view they should have been replaced with
substantial changes to the business logic forgetting the fact they
were a maintenance nightmare.  The payroll programs with this
construct were replaced by a package in the late 1980's.  The use of
ALTER GO TO with the current IBM COBOL compilers would be inefficient
given the interesting things they do with PERFORM.  I now avoid GO TO
if at all possible, both for the same reason I used it in the first
place - efficiency and because of the maintenance nightmare.  I know
one programmer who inherited my payroll programs went to my boss at
the time with the worry that he had a problem because he was beginning
to understand them.  That person and his office partner were described
by my boss as the only two people he knew who could hold separate
conversations and answer each other.  I respected the abilities of all
three people mentioned.

>Put more succinctly, in two words:  Blame Dijkstra!
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: interesting use of NEXT SENTENCE vs. CONTINUE

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2005-06-06T08:07:56-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d81osr$5ra$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<3g8q52Fb2itjU1@individual.net> <1117863336.941688.184770@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <fvboe.185329$uu5.62495@fe05.news.easynews.com> <1117874000.565517.100420@g44g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <d7s7o9$6ol$1@panix5.panix.com> <3gfukvFc4h3qU1@individual.net>`

```

"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3gfukvFc4h3qU1@individual.net...

> My advice: Don't use NEXT SENTENCE.... ever. It is a stupid and
unnecessary
> inclusion in the COBOL language.

The authors of the current COBOL standard agree that its use should be
discouraged.  Support for it is  retained so that existing programs
containing it will continue to compile, but NEXT SENTENCE is marked as an
"archaic element" of standard COBOL in all contexts in which it is allowed
by the standard (IF and SEARCH statements).  The standard doesn't
characterize it as "stupid"; it does say it "can be confusing" and that its
function is commonly misunderstood.  "The CONTINUE statement and scope
delimiters can be used to accomplish the same functionality and such
constructs are clearer and less prone to error."  (ISO/IEC 1989:2005 page
833, Archaic and obsolete language element lists).

    -Chuck Stevens
```

compilers with their built-in macro/preprocessor COULD pass the COBOL 
certification tests.  However, if I had stated that this made them a "COBOL 
compiler" - I think people would have been right to question my use of the term 
and its definition.

former
> extension has now been incorporated within the standard, but I'm not the
> standards expert you are.   I'd hate to discover that I've been lying to
my
> co-workers about it though.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: interesting use of NEXT SENTENCE vs. CONTINUE

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2005-06-06T19:13:07+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7f1pe.248825$uu5.45196@fe05.news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<3g8q52Fb2itjU1@individual.net> <1117765632.634020.104870@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <d7p7pr$87a$1@panix5.panix.com> <1117828199.855793.254140@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <d7ql6s$pne$1@panix5.panix.com> <1117851012.574197.212860@g47g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <Faaoe.239233$CV1.4226@fe02.news.easynews.com> <d81m6j$ph9$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
GoBACK is in the '02 Standard.  The point was that the previous standards did 
NOT include it as one of the "Standard" statements.  If (especially 
out-of-context) it seemed as if *I* were saying that extensions were disallowed, 
that was NOT my point (and I do NOT agree with it).

My point is that the Standard (current, past, and draft) *ALL* allow for 
extensions - both to the "positively" and "negatively" worded requirements, 
e.g.

  YOU MAY have an extension adding COMP-3 and GoBack (to the '85 Standard)
  YOU MAY  have an extension allowing SYNC at a group level and IF NUMERIC of 
non-DISPLAY items (to the '85 Standard)
  YOU MAY have an extension allowing NEXT SENTENCE in the same level as an IF 
with END-IF or a SEARCH with END-SEARCH.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: interesting use of NEXT SENTENCE vs. CONTINUE

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2005-06-06T11:57:24-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d81rp4$1co$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<3g8q52Fb2itjU1@individual.net> <1117897222.358113.249260@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <d7sqp5$93g$1@panix5.panix.com> <d81mui$q7f$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
In article <d81mui$q7f$1@peabody.colorado.edu>,
Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
>
>On  4-Jun-2005, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>Sometimes it is, sometimes it isn't.

Nothing is always, Mr Brazee, including this statement.

>And sometimes it is is necessary to
>determine how to answer.

It is possible, in my experience, to determine further data without 
answering a question with a question... which is, as noted above, no 
answer at all.  The reasons I use in considering it as such I posted a 
while back... ah:

<http://groups-beta.google.com/group/comp.lang.cobol/msg/967c9d77a4959544?dmode=source&hl=en>

--begin quoted text:

I thought I'd addressed this here but perhaps not, it might have been in 
another venue.

Quite openly and honestly: I find that for the overwhelming majority of 
cases answering a question with a question is a stall, a dodge or some way 
to avoid/evade the original query; the 'air' that it carries is 'I cannot 
answer your question so... I'll ask one of you and if you cannot answer it 
then in some way we'll both be even and our arguments will cancel each 
other out, maybe I can even assume a bit of smug superiority by having 
posed the last, unanswerable question.'

Note that I said 'overwhelming majority of cases', not 'all'... but I have 
*never* found a case where the answering question could not be phrased in 
a manner that would turn the dialogue away from the seriocomic interchange 
you posted into something a bit closer to what I would call 'rational 
discourse'.  Consider the differences between:

'Yeah?  Well, what about (condition)?'
'Whaddaya mean, (condition)?'

... and ...

'Yeah?  Well, what about (condition)?'
'I cannot address that until you make it clear what criteria you are using 
to establish the existence of (condition)?'

Now turning the discord of Duelling Interrogatories into the harmony of 
Rational Discourse might require something called 'honesty' and 
'integrity' as the tactics involve using such phrases as 'I don't know' or 
'I cannot address that' or 'I cannot see the relationship'... but if such 
things are uncomfortable they can, with a bit of practise, be hidden 
without forcing folks to fall back into mutual queries.

This having been said - that in the overwhelming majority of cases it is 
used as an evasion instead of an answer and that there are, as noted 
above, structures readily available to render its use unnecessary - then 
the conclusion is that the technique of answering a question with a 
question is treated as an ALTER and should be structured out of existence.

('Yeah?  What about replacing it with a called Assembley module?' 'I don't 
know what I would say about that without reading the code; if you have it 
available I would be most interested in reading it *first* and *then* 
rendering an opinion... but I *do* know that replacing things in such 
manner usually does not eliminate the structural flaws, it merely shifts 
them.')

--end quoted text

(note - in quoting this I noticed an error in my punctuation, ''I cannot 
address that until you make it clear what criteria you are using to 
establish the existence of (condition)?'' should have been rendered as ''I 
cannot address that until you make it clear what criteria you are using to 
establish the existence of (condition).'')

But wait... there's more!  From even longer back, in another forum:

<http://groups-beta.google.com/group/comp.software.year-2000/msg/34b3233328d534e1?dmode=source&hl=en>

--begin quoted text:

Different folks enjoy, appreciate and benefit from different styles of 
interaction, of course... one size fits none and all that; it is good that 
you enjoy this sort of interaction and find benefit in it.

Me, I've found that not everyone is a rabbi (what a surprise!) and that 
the tactic of answering a question with a question is employed less often 
to teach and more often to dodge... this observation is not, granted, 
universally true (no observation is, of course... including this one) but 
I've found it often enough to be the case.  At times, of course, it can 
get downright infantile:

A: 'What are the suppositions and logic you employed in reaching this 
conclusion?'

B: 'What suppositions and logic would *you* employ to reach this
conclusion?'

A: 'I cannot see *any* set of suppositions and logic which might be 
employed to reach this conclusion, hence my asking you this.'

B: 'Well don't blame *me* because *you* can't see things... you must be a 
poopie-head, nyah nyah nyah!'


... and such a situation, at time, might just possibly be avoided by the 
judicious invoking of 'Answering a question with a question is no answer 
at all; please be so kind as to address the query.'

--end quoted text

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: interesting use of NEXT SENTENCE vs. CONTINUE

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2005-06-06T16:47:10-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<98c29$42a4c45b$45491c57$26195@KNOLOGY.NET>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<d81rp4$1co$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<3g8q52Fb2itjU1@individual.net> <1117897222.358113.249260@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <d7sqp5$93g$1@panix5.panix.com> <d81mui$q7f$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <d81rp4$1co$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
> Quite openly and honestly: I find that for the overwhelming majority of 
> cases answering a question with a question is a stall, a dodge or some way 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> posed the last, unanswerable question.'

I'm certainly not in the position to question your experience, much less 
reinterpret it for you.  I believe, though, that some folks employing 
this technique are much less shady than you're esteeming them.

As a programmer, when someone comes up to me and asks me a "how do you" 
question, I often do not just give them an answer.  Rather, I'll ask 
them a question that enables them to draw on knowledge they already 
have, to deduce a solution (that's usually accurate).  Once they "do 
their homework", so to speak, I'll then show them in the manual, or 
wherever is applicable, where their answer is found.

By doing this, they not only have the confidence to go on to make other 
deductions themselves, but they also become familiar with the supporting 
documentation that we use.  When it comes down to it, I'm probably no 
better a programmer than most other guys in the shop - I just know where 
to look for the answers.

(When I was assigned to our configuration management system, which used 
ASP and SQL Server, this guy drove me insane - every question I'd ask 
him he'd answer with four letters - "MSDN".  I thought my head would 
explode, I got so frustrated with him at times.  But, you know, I can't 
remember the last time I had a VB question that I had to ask someone 
else about!)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: interesting use of NEXT SENTENCE vs. CONTINUE

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2005-06-04T19:08:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<z_moe.52$Wv.36@fe08.news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<3g8q52Fb2itjU1@individual.net> <1117828199.855793.254140@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <d7ql6s$pne$1@panix5.panix.com> <1117851012.574197.212860@g47g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <d7s75k$q3c$1@panix5.panix.com> <1117897222.358113.249260@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`

```
the exact words from the '85 Standard are:

"The NUMERIC test cannot be used with an item whose data description describes 
the item as alphabetic or as a group item composed of elementary items whose 
data description indicates the presence of operational sign(s)."

So, to me that means "must not be specified"

For an example of where the Standard uses those EXACT words and Micro Focus (for 
example) has an extension, see:

"The clauses SYNCHRONIZED, ... must not be specified except for an elementary 
data item."

(MF allows it at a group level)

   ***

also:

 "The OCCURS clause must not be specified in a data description entry that: ..."

MF allows it at the 01-level.  MF and IBM allows them to be "nested" (See MF 
ODOSLIDE directive)

***

Also,

 "The VALUE clause must not be specified in the Linkage Section"

Allowed by MF, IBM, and many other compilers.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: interesting use of NEXT SENTENCE vs. CONTINUE

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-06-04T14:08:15-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1117919295.721873.96000@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<z_moe.52$Wv.36@fe08.news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<3g8q52Fb2itjU1@individual.net> <1117828199.855793.254140@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <d7ql6s$pne$1@panix5.panix.com> <1117851012.574197.212860@g47g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <d7s75k$q3c$1@panix5.panix.com> <1117897222.358113.249260@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <z_moe.52$Wv.36@fe08.news.easynews.com>`

```
> So, to me that means "must not be specified"

I cannot be held responsible for how you misinterpret particular
statements. In fact I insist that I _must_not_ be held responsible.

I, for example _can_ see that there is a distinction between:

"You can not walk on water".
"You must not walk on the grass".
"You should not walk barefoot on broken glass".

If you think that they convey the same imperitive, then try switching
the phrases around and see if they still make sense. If you cannot see
the distinction, then you cannot.

> an example of where the Standard uses those EXACT words

At last you have stopped just making things up.

> "The clauses SYNCHRONIZED, ... must not be specified ...
> "The OCCURS clause must not be specified ...
> "The VALUE clause must not be ...

Thank you for pointing those out, I will take these particular issues
into consideration should I ever be bothered to make up a new list of
things that I think abuse the language.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Postive and Negative requirements (was: interesting use of NEXT SENTENCE vs. CONTINUE

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2005-06-04T22:35:22+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<K0qoe.263183$CV1.64015@fe02.news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<3g8q52Fb2itjU1@individual.net> <1117828199.855793.254140@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <d7ql6s$pne$1@panix5.panix.com> <1117851012.574197.212860@g47g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <d7s75k$q3c$1@panix5.panix.com> <1117897222.358113.249260@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <z_moe.52$Wv.36@fe08.news.easynews.com> <1117919295.721873.96000@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`

```

"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message 
news:1117919295.721873.96000@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com...
>> So, to me that means "must not be specified"
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Within the COBOL Standard ('85 and '02 - probably before and probably will be 
after), there are SOME *requirements* written positively and some that are 
written negatively.  These are ALL "requirements" of the language specification 
and all have "equivalent" wording that could be provided using the opposite 
technique.  For example, (from the '02 Standard as it is easier for me to 
search)

"A given literal shall not be continued with more than one form of 
continuation."

    which COULD be worded as

"A given literal shall be continued with only one form of continuation."

 * * * *
or

"All the characters forming a multiple-character floating indicator shall be 
specified on the same line."

which could be worded as

"The characters forming a multiple-character floating indicator shall not be 
specified on multiple lines."

For a DETAILED description of what these mean (and this is an ISO requirement - 
not a COBOL requirement), see the discussion of:

  "Verbal forms for the expression of provisions"

On page 21 of the '02 Standard.  (You will notice that "must not" is no longer 
even permitted to be used within the Standard - and there is no such wording for 
NEXT SENTENCE and END-IF in the only "currently acknowledged" ANSI/ISO COBOL 
Standard).

These may (or may not) be how YOU use the language in other contexts, but this 
is the way the COBOL Standard uses it.

FYI,
  For END-IF and  "NEXT SENTENCE" the '02 Standard handles this via the syntax 
(general format) diagram - not by a syntax rule.  However, the following wording 
is used for NEXT SENTENCE and SEARCH,

 "If the END-SEARCH phrase is specified, the NEXT SENTENCE phrase shall not be 
specified."
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Postive and Negative requirements (was: interesting use of NEXT SENTENCE vs. CONTINUE

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-06-04T16:33:44-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1117928023.972656.276500@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<K0qoe.263183$CV1.64015@fe02.news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<3g8q52Fb2itjU1@individual.net> <1117828199.855793.254140@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <d7ql6s$pne$1@panix5.panix.com> <1117851012.574197.212860@g47g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <d7s75k$q3c$1@panix5.panix.com> <1117897222.358113.249260@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <z_moe.52$Wv.36@fe08.news.easynews.com> <1117919295.721873.96000@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <K0qoe.263183$CV1.64015@fe02.news.easynews.com>`

```
> which COULD be worded as

> "A given literal shall be continued with only one form of continuation."

No. Wrong. Your version implies that continuation is a requirement:
'The literal shall be continued', the 'only one form' is then only a
limitation on how this requirement is fullfilled.

> which could be worded as ...

I think that the committe have, for the most part, worded the items as
they wish to have them having considered what they actually intended.
I doubt that they would agree that your versions are equivalent,
especially as you have admitted that you don't see the difference
between not mentioning something and disallowing it.

> FYI,
> For END-IF and  "NEXT SENTENCE" the '02 Standard handles this via the syntax
> (general format) diagram - not by a syntax rule.

I am pleased to hear that. They did have to admit that the '85 rule
could lead to a conditional IF statement with NEXT SENTENCE being
embeded in an imperitive IF and thus bypassing the intent of the
prohibition. I trust this new formulation is designed to eliminate
that.

> However, the following wording
> is used for NEXT SENTENCE and SEARCH,

> "If the END-SEARCH phrase is specified, the NEXT SENTENCE phrase shall not be
> specified."

One may assume that there was no need to change this because no one
contrived to misuse this within the wording of the standard.
```

>> which COULD be worded as
>
<snip>

 (general format) diagram - not by a syntax rule.
>
> I am pleased to hear that. They did have to admit that the '85 rule
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

No, it is even clear that it is INTENTIONAL that the following is VALID 
(conforming) source code:

If A-field = "A"
   IF A-field = "A"
          Next Sentence
   Else
        Display "Never Reached"
Else
   Display "Not A"
End-IF
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Postive and Negative requirements (was: interesting use of NEXT SENTENCE vs. CONTINUE

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-06-04T17:58:51-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1117933131.539990.23360@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<wZqoe.972$Wv.580@fe08.news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<3g8q52Fb2itjU1@individual.net> <1117828199.855793.254140@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <d7ql6s$pne$1@panix5.panix.com> <1117851012.574197.212860@g47g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <d7s75k$q3c$1@panix5.panix.com> <1117897222.358113.249260@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <z_moe.52$Wv.36@fe08.news.easynews.com> <1117919295.721873.96000@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <K0qoe.263183$CV1.64015@fe02.news.easynews.com> <1117928023.972656.276500@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <wZqoe.972$Wv.580@fe08.news.easynews.com>`

```
> No, it is even clear that it is INTENTIONAL that the following is VALID
> (conforming) source code:

You have made the assertion that it is intentional in the past, but so
far you have not provided any actual evidence on which you based this.

It is clear that one can contrive the rules in such a way that they do
not explicitly disallow it, and I understand that the committee have
agreed that it is not disallowed by the actual wording.

It is a large leap, however, to make the claim that it was intentional.
This requires that they actually considered the issue (for which I have
seen no evidence) and then decided to deliberately allow it (for which
I have seen no evidence) rather than, say, not being able to formulate
an acceptable rule that would disallow it without impacting on other
areas.

In fact I would say that the change in this area in '02 may be as a
result of this contrived avoidance of the rule.

If it is so clear then you should be able to quote the wording
_from_the_standard_ (and not MF notations) as to what make you think it
was intended.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Postive and Negative requirements (was: interesting use of NEXT SENTENCE vs. CONTINUE

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2005-06-05T02:30:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Vstoe.2181$Wv.1393@fe08.news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<3g8q52Fb2itjU1@individual.net> <1117828199.855793.254140@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <d7ql6s$pne$1@panix5.panix.com> <1117851012.574197.212860@g47g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <d7s75k$q3c$1@panix5.panix.com> <1117897222.358113.249260@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <z_moe.52$Wv.36@fe08.news.easynews.com> <1117919295.721873.96000@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <K0qoe.263183$CV1.64015@fe02.news.easynews.com> <1117928023.972656.276500@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <wZqoe.972$Wv.580@fe08.news.easynews.com> <1117933131.539990.23360@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`

```
For the '02 Standard, read the "general format" on page 455 where the (clarify 
if it was uncler - which the committee did not think it was - in the '85 
Standard) that NEXT SENTENCE may not be used WHEN IMMEDIATELY followed by END-IF 
(or an ELSE imperative-statement-2 END-IF).  In cases where the IF statement 
does NOT (itself) include the END-IF phrase, then it is (clearly and always) 
valid (conforming).

As far as the (now no longer current) '85 Standard, you need to read the 
response to the interpretation request where they talk about the rules that 
mention:

  "An END-IF phrase at the same level of nesting"
(in one rule - talking about the "scope" of an IF Statement versus nested IF's) 
and then say
  "If the END-IF phrase is specified, the NEXT SENTENCE phrase must not be 
specified."

where it is clear (to them - if not to you) that this is NOT a rule talking 
about nested IF's.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Postive and Negative requirements (was: interesting use of NEXT SENTENCE vs. CONTINUE

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-06-04T22:07:59-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1117948079.117667.172630@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<Vstoe.2181$Wv.1393@fe08.news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<3g8q52Fb2itjU1@individual.net> <1117828199.855793.254140@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <d7ql6s$pne$1@panix5.panix.com> <1117851012.574197.212860@g47g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <d7s75k$q3c$1@panix5.panix.com> <1117897222.358113.249260@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <z_moe.52$Wv.36@fe08.news.easynews.com> <1117919295.721873.96000@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <K0qoe.263183$CV1.64015@fe02.news.easynews.com> <1117928023.972656.276500@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <wZqoe.972$Wv.580@fe08.news.easynews.com> <1117933131.539990.23360@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <Vstoe.2181$Wv.1393@fe08.news.easynews.com>`

```
> where it is clear (to them - if not to you) that this is NOT a rule talking
> about nested IF's.

Yes, but your claim was that this was intended when it is actually
indistinguishable from them merely not having considered this and/or
not wanting to formulate such a rule as it would complexify other
issues.

I entirely agree that it was intended that a conditional IF statement
be allowed nested within an  imperitive IF, but if they intended to
allow this to have a NEXT SENTENCE then why did they bother explicitly
disallowing it within the imperitive ?

I agree that the clarification does state that the particular rule only
applies to the one level of imperitive IF. That in itself does not mean
that it was intended to be limited in that way, only that it turned out
to be so limited.

The intent in the '85 rule is pure conjecture on your part, because you
want it to be so. I see nothing that makes it other than an oversight
in '85, even in the clarification. In that and now in '02 they have to
live with what that may have allowed inadvertantly.
```

"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message 
news:1117948079.117667.172630@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com...
>> where it is clear (to them - if not to you) that this is NOT a rule talking
>> about nested IF's.
<snip>
>
> The intent in the '85 rule is pure conjecture on your part, because you
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Richard,
  How many J4 meetings have you attended?  How many WG4 meetings have you 
attended?  How many Codasyl COBOL Committee meetings have you attended?

Were you involved (attended) the J4 meetings where the interpretation request 
specifically about this syntax was discussed?

I was there (at all of these) and can assure you that (in my mind) there was NO 
DOUBT that this "feature" was intentional for SOME - (not all) participants in 
the DEVELOPMENT of the '85 Standard.

One part of the history that you may not be familiar with is the fact that the 
JOD *explicitly* did allow a NEXT SENTENCE with an END-IF (in the same "level" 
of the IF statement).  There was discussion about this during the development of 
the '85 Standard and the decision was (eventually) NOT to allow this.  HOWEVER, 
there was also discussion about the fact that there is one AND ONLY ONE place 
within the Standard ('85 or '74) where a "conditional statement" MAY be nested 
within another statement - and that is within either the IF (THEN) or the ELSE 
portions of an IF statement.  Furthermore, there was discussion about whether a 
2nd thru Nth "conditional phrase" (e.g. ELSE, WHEN, NOT AT END, etc) would or 
would not "terminate" a nested conditional statement.  It was discussed and 
decided that it would - HOWEVER, as only the IF statement could have a nested 
conditional statement, it was decided that "ELSE" (and only ELSE) would be 
allowed to terminate a nested conditional statement (and this INCLUDED the 
"conditional" format of an IF statement - with a NEXT SENTENCE and without an 
END-IF).

I will be happy to admit (and agree to it) that SOME members of the committees 
drafting the '85 Standard did NOT want this (and it is even possible that some 
didn't really understand it) - but there is no doubt that the committee "as a 
whole" DID consider this - and make the rules as they did.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1118032405.646051.54220@g47g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<H9woe.15419$W62.4995@fe10.news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<3g8q52Fb2itjU1@individual.net> <1117828199.855793.254140@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <d7ql6s$pne$1@panix5.panix.com> <1117851012.574197.212860@g47g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <d7s75k$q3c$1@panix5.panix.com> <1117897222.358113.249260@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <z_moe.52$Wv.36@fe08.news.easynews.com> <1117919295.721873.96000@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <K0qoe.263183$CV1.64015@fe02.news.easynews.com> <1117928023.972656.276500@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <wZqoe.972$Wv.580@fe08.news.easynews.com> <1117933131.539990.23360@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <Vstoe.2181$Wv.1393@fe08.news.easynews.com> <1117948079.117667.172630@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <H9woe.15419$W62.4995@fe10.news.easynews.com>`

```
> One part of the history that you may not be familiar with is the fact that the
> JOD *explicitly* did allow a NEXT SENTENCE with an END-IF (in the same "level"
> of the IF statement).

Actually I am familiar with this, and it does your case no good at all.
In the JOD the NEXT SENTENCE was equivalent to CONTINUE. MicroFocus
implemented this and had to change when the standard made the
definition of NEXT SENTENCE into 'the next full stop'.

The result is that MF has an OLDNEXTSENTENCE compiler directive to
cater for code written to the JOD.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Postive and Negative requirements (was: interesting use of NEXT SENTENCE vs. CONTINUE

_(thread depth: 20)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2005-06-06T19:29:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bu1pe.58080$Wv.42324@fe08.news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<3g8q52Fb2itjU1@individual.net> <1117828199.855793.254140@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <d7ql6s$pne$1@panix5.panix.com> <1117851012.574197.212860@g47g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <d7s75k$q3c$1@panix5.panix.com> <1117897222.358113.249260@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <z_moe.52$Wv.36@fe08.news.easynews.com> <1117919295.721873.96000@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <K0qoe.263183$CV1.64015@fe02.news.easynews.com> <1117928023.972656.276500@g49g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <wZqoe.972$Wv.580@fe08.news.easynews.com> <1117933131.539990.23360@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <Vstoe.2181$Wv.1393@fe08.news.easynews.com> <1117948079.117667.172630@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <H9woe.15419$W62.4995@fe10.news.easynews.com> <1118032405.646051.54220@g47g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`

```
ACTUALLY, I was working with Micro Focus when the OLNEXTSENTENCE directive was 
introduced.  The compiler writer who made it equivalent to "CONTINUE" admitted 
that he was in error and that the JOD and the (draft at that time) '85 Standard 
*NEVER* said that.  NEXT SENTENCE has *always* been defined (in every Standard) 
as "going to the next sentence" and NOT to the next statement.
```

Yeah, I strongly suspect J4 was being diplomatic when it indicated that it
was the *users* who were sometimes confused about where control proceeds on
a NEXT SENTENCE ...

I also have this fairly strong suspicion that there just might be another
vendor or two aside from MF out there whose COBOL85 implementations
erroneously generated code to transfer control to the next *statement* in
cases like this ...  ;-(

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: interesting use of NEXT SENTENCE vs. CONTINUE

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2005-06-06T14:49:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d81nq2$qq3$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<3g8q52Fb2itjU1@individual.net> <1117828199.855793.254140@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <d7ql6s$pne$1@panix5.panix.com> <d81lvr$p3v$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <d81mff$g5g$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```

On  6-Jun-2005, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

> >Although including it within an IF END-IF pair as the code that started this
> >thread does not.   (On my computer it is called an Extension).
>
> So, it seems, what on your computer is called an Extension qualifies,
> under at least one view, as 'abuse'.

Apparently.   I can see someone setting up standards that disallow extensions
that are explicitly proscribed by the standard.    Breaking a standard has been
called "abuse" in my experience.

But in evaluating why to do this, it would be because:

1.   I don't expect that extension to be available when I upgrade my computer.
2.   I expect my code to be portable if we go to a new computer.
3.   Somebody created a standard and nobody knows why, but it's a standard and
changing it will take man-months of effort.

Personally I don't believe in #2 at all.   I've done too many conversions in the
past.   Even changing databases with the same type of computer require
re-writes.

#2 seems unlikely but possible.

I've had lots of experience with #3 being true.
```

Richard<riplin@Azonic.co.nz> 6/2/2005 1:13:04 PM >>>
>Frank Swarbrick wrote:
>> *    *************************************************************
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>be specified."""

True enough, but it's an "extention" allowed by IBM COBOL for VSE/ESA (and
other compilers, I'm sure).  From my reference manual:
" Note:   END-IF can be specified with NEXT SENTENCE as an IBM extension."

Not that I think it's a good idea.  I don't.

>> The "interesting" code is between the last to lines of all asterisks. 
It
>> was obviously added in after the original code, and was obviously done
in
>> order to avoid adding a new GO TO such as:
>
>There is no need at all to add any GO TO.

Please clarify.  Of course I did show an example of how the entire

NEXT
>> SENTENCE in this way.
>

someone actually using it.

Frank

#### ↳ Re: interesting use of NEXT SENTENCE vs. CONTINUE

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-06-03T12:30:47+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3g9mlpFba9j1U1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<3g8q52Fb2itjU1@individual.net>`

```

"Frank Swarbrick" <Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com> wrote in message
news:3g8q52Fb2itjU1@individual.net...
> With all the discussion of the differences between NEXT SENTENCE and
> CONTINUE over the years I've been reading comp.lang.cobol I just could not
…[54 more quoted lines elided]…
> (Basically, for VPAY card accounts this exception is generated after 41
days
> instead of the 7 day period for all other account SVC types.)
>
> Actually, now that I look at it that is somewhat hard to understand, so I
> guess I can see why he chose a separate IF statement.  But the CONTINUE
vs.
> NEXT SENTENCE thing is just nutty.
>
> Anyway, just found it amusing that someone thought it was clever to use
NEXT
> SENTENCE in this way.
>
Thanks for posting this, Frank.

It has generated some lively interest and, for myself, it made me stop and
think.

I couldn't understand why it left a bad taste in my mouth (in more than 20
years programming, all over the world, I've seen many different COBOL
'cultures' , but never this) and then I realised (after about 20 minutes)
why this has been considered necessary...

It isn't just about obviating a GO TO.

It is because two different styles have been MIXED.

And because installation standards have probably prohibited the use of
negated conditions.

I wondered why it is that, if given the same logical requirement, I WOULDN'T
code it that way.

it's because....

1.  I use EITHER scope delimiters OR full stops; never both. (OK, COBOL
requires a full stop before a section or paragraph header but  I see that as
syntax rather than logic...). Mixing 'old' and 'new' COBOL does a disservice
to both.
2.  I optimize logic before coding it and use whatever Booleans help me
achieve that.

Here's what I would do with the above...

 *    *************************************************************
 *    * PRODUCE EXCEPTION FOR ACCOUNTS THAT HAVE NOT HAD ANY    ***
 *    * TRANSACTIONS, NO CASH RESERVE AND HAVE BEEN OPEN AT     ***
 *    * LEAST SEVEN DAYS                                        ***
 *    *************************************************************
      IF NOT (CURR-BAL = ZERO
              AND DATE-LAST-TXN = ZERO
              AND DATE-LAST-STMT = ZERO
              AND DATE-OPEN < DATE-6-DAYS-AGO
              AND NOT-IN-SWEEP-GRP
              AND NOT MSTR-CLOSING-ACCT-OD
              AND NOT MSTR-CHARGE-OFF-ACCT
              AND NOT MSTR-OLD-CHG-OFF-ACCT
              AND NOT MSTR-INTERNAL-BANK
              AND RSRV-CURR-BAL = ZERO
              AND RSRV-LIMIT = ZERO
              AND NO-D13-GENERAL-MEMO)
                       CONTINUE
        ELSE
 *    *************************************************************
              IF  VPAY-CARD-SVC   AND
                            NOT (DATE-OPEN < DATE-40-DAYS-AGO)
                      CONTINUE
              ELSE

 *    *************************************************************
                     MOVE 'M'                TO REPORT-CODE
                    +058 TO REPORT-RSN
                    PERFORM 6200-EXCEPT-OUTPUT THRU
                                       6200-EXCEPT-OUTPUT-EXIT
                    MOVE 'C'   TO    MSTR-CODE-OVERDRAFT
                    GO TO PROCESS-LARGE-BAL-CHANGE  *> I'd probably find a
way to remove this...
              END-IF
       END-IF

I believe this achieves the same logic as the original (obviously, I can't
check it, so I could be wrong) but it is simpler, and it should be COBOL
compliant.

Pete.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: interesting use of NEXT SENTENCE vs. CONTINUE

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2005-06-03T14:18:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d7porq$1nu$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<3g8q52Fb2itjU1@individual.net> <3g9mlpFba9j1U1@individual.net>`

```

On  2-Jun-2005, "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

> it's because....
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> to both.

I use both.   I don't consider full stops to be "mixed styles".    But I use a
pre-compiler that puts in some if/else logic and like to make it very clear to
maintenance programmers that full stops are there.

> 2.  I optimize logic before coding it and use whatever Booleans help me
> achieve that.

I do this too - except I also believe in the concept of maintaining code with
minimal changes.   Often this results in logic that isn't the way I would code
that paragraph if I were starting from scratch.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: interesting use of NEXT SENTENCE vs. CONTINUE

- **From:** "Frank Swarbrick" <Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com>
- **Date:** 2005-06-03T08:28:41-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3gb7otFbeumaU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<3g8q52Fb2itjU1@individual.net> <3g9mlpFba9j1U1@individual.net>`

```
Top post only (my newsreader sucks)...

I do indeed like your solution!  Although at first glance a NOT of a NOT
could be confusing.  In fact, I'd probably make one additional change:
IF  VPAY-CARD-SVC   AND
                           DATE-OPEN >= DATE-40-DAYS-AGO

One big problem I had when looking at this is getting straight in my mind
just exactly what "IF DATE-OPEN < DATE-6-DAYS-AGO" means in "English".  It
means "If the account was opened *more* than seven days ago, but it
*appears* at first glance to mean "If the account was opened less than six
days ago".

Of course changing the 7-day ago log to "AND (NOT DATE-OPEN >=
DATE-7-DAYS-AGO)" doesn't look right either. 
What about "AND DATE-OPEN NOT >= DATE-7-DAYS AGO".
Nah, still confusing.

Anyway, beside the point...

As for our installation standards, while negated conditions are somewhat
discouraged, they are not forbidden, and they are used when the programmer
decides it makes the "cleanest" looking and easiest to understand code.  As
for what this particular programmer was thinking at the time he did this
particular coding, I have no idea.

I personally don't like to use full-stops except where they are required. 
But this applies only to new programs, or at least new paragraphs within an
existing program.  If I'm modifying an existing paragraph I try to add code
in the same "style" as the existing code.  I've seen people who don't follow
that rule screw things up too many times.

Oh, and that final GO TO?  Good luck getting rid of that guy.  Take a look
at these labels:
1000-PROCESS-DDA-MST.

1350-RSRV-FEE.

1350-INCREMENT-RSRV-FEE-DATE.

1350-RSRV-FEE-EXIT.

1400-PROCESS-DDA-STAT-FIELDS.

1400-PROC-DDA-STAT-FIELD-EXIT.

1405-CALC-WIRE-FEES-WAIVED.

1405-CALC-PCB-WIRE-FEES-WAIVED.

1405-CALC-PHN-WIRE-FEES-WAIVED.

1406-ACCUM-WAIVED-WIRE-FEES.

1406-EXIT-SECTION-1405.

1500-SWEEP-CASH-RESERVES-PMTS.

1500-SWEEP-CASH-RSRV-PMTS-EXIT.

1600-PROCESS-DDA-MST-RADV.

1601-CONTINUE-RSRV-INT.

1602-CONTINUE-RSRV-INT.

1610-PROC-DDA-M-RSRV-AUTO-PAY.

1612-PROCESS-NON-ACCRUALS.

1612-PROCESS-NON-ACCRUALS-EXIT.

1615-PROCESS-FINANCE-CHARGE.

1620-PROCESS-DDA-MST-SERVCG.

1630-PROCESS-DDA-MST-STMT1.

1640-PROCESS-DDA-MST-STMT2.

1640-AFTER-DEFERRED.

1640-CONTINUE.

1641-PDN-LOAD-LOOP.

1642-CONTINUE.

1650-PROCESS-DDA-MST-UPDATE.

1655-CONTINUE.

1660-PROCESS-DDA-MST-UPDATE.

1700-PROCESS-LARGE-BAL-CHANGE.

1800-PROCESS-ENDING-TOTALS-ADD.

1800-RSRV-FIXED-RATE-EXPIRE.

1800-CHECK-FOR-CHARGE-BACKS.

1800-ROTATE-CASH-OUT-2-3.

1800-CHECK-END-OF-MONTH.

1900-WRITE-1419-FILE.

1900-CONTINUE-WRITE-1419.

1000-PROCESS-DDA-MST-EXIT.

Basically, there are several places (outside of these labels) that do
PERFORM 1000-PROCESS-DDA-MST THRU 1000-PROCESS-DDA-MST-EXIT.

And within this huge set of paragraphs there are GO TO's to downstream
labels, there are PERFORM...THRU's of paragraphs imbedded within (for
example: "PERFORM 1400-PROCESS-DDA-STAT-FIELDS THRU
1400-PROC-DDA-STAT-FIELD-EXIT."), and there are PERFORM...THRU's of many
paragraphs that are outside of the 1000- start to 1000- exit labels.

There are 4533 lines in this huge section of paragraphs (and the entire
procedure division is 26855 lines, not including a few procedure division
copybooks).  Now keep in mind this program was first implemented in 1978 and
possibly based on a predecessor version that was written in RPG-II, so I
tend to give it some leeway...  :-)

All that being said, the program is still relatively simple to understand,
for the most part.  At least the paragraph labels generally describe what
the code that follows is doing (at least to some extent).

Frank


--- 
Frank Swarbrick
Senior Developer/Analyst - Mainframe Applications
FirstBank Data Corporation - Lakewood, CO  USA

I wondered why it is that, if given the same logical requirement, I

it's because....

disservice
to both.
2.  I optimize logic before coding it and use whatever Booleans help me

Here's what I would do with the above...

 *    *************************************************************
 *    * PRODUCE EXCEPTION FOR ACCOUNTS THAT HAVE NOT HAD ANY    ***
 *    * TRANSACTIONS, NO CASH RESERVE AND HAVE BEEN OPEN AT     ***
 *    * LEAST SEVEN DAYS                                        ***

              AND DATE-LAST-TXN = ZERO

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: interesting use of NEXT SENTENCE vs. CONTINUE

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2005-06-03T08:49:56-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d7pu75$kdt$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<3g8q52Fb2itjU1@individual.net> <3g9mlpFba9j1U1@individual.net> <3gb7otFbeumaU1@individual.net>`

```

"Frank Swarbrick" <Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com> wrote in message
news:3gb7otFbeumaU1@individual.net...

> What about "AND DATE-OPEN NOT >= DATE-7-DAYS AGO".
> Nah, still confusing.

Also not standard COBOL.  ISO/IEC 1989:2002 (which is the standard in which
">=" and "<=" were introduced as relational operators) expressly prohibits
"NOT" before them.  ">=" is syntactically synonymous with "GREATER THAN OR
EQUAL TO" which is in turn logically synonymous with "NOT LESS THAN", and
"NOT NOT LESS THAN" is likewise prohibited.

The rules and syntax diagrams are clarified some in the proposed draft
standard (as part of the addition of "<>" as synonymous with "NOT =" and
"NOT EQUAL TO") by moving the "NOT <relational-operator>" combinations from
the "simple-relational-operator" category to the
"extended-relational-operator" category; NOT is precluded before the latter.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: interesting use of NEXT SENTENCE vs. CONTINUE

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Frank Swarbrick" <Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com>
- **Date:** 2005-06-03T12:59:24-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3gbnkdFbov82U1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<3g8q52Fb2itjU1@individual.net> <3g9mlpFba9j1U1@individual.net> <3gb7otFbeumaU1@individual.net> <d7pu75$kdt$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
Interesting.  Did not know that.  Don't think I would have ever actually
used it anyway, but...

>>> Chuck Stevens<charles.stevens@unisys.com> 6/3/2005 9:49:56 AM >>>

"Frank Swarbrick" <Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com> wrote in message
news:3gb7otFbeumaU1@individual.net...

> What about "AND DATE-OPEN NOT >= DATE-7-DAYS AGO".
> Nah, still confusing.

Also not standard COBOL.  ISO/IEC 1989:2002 (which is the standard in which
">=" and "<=" were introduced as relational operators) expressly prohibits
"NOT" before them.  ">=" is syntactically synonymous with "GREATER THAN OR
EQUAL TO" which is in turn logically synonymous with "NOT LESS THAN", and
"NOT NOT LESS THAN" is likewise prohibited.

The rules and syntax diagrams are clarified some in the proposed draft
standard (as part of the addition of "<>" as synonymous with "NOT =" and
"NOT EQUAL TO") by moving the "NOT <relational-operator>" combinations from
the "simple-relational-operator" category to the
"extended-relational-operator" category; NOT is precluded before the
latter.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

#### ↳ Re: interesting use of NEXT SENTENCE vs. CONTINUE

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2005-06-02T20:45:03-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<63356$429fb60c$45491c57$1754@KNOLOGY.NET>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<3g8q52Fb2itjU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<3g8q52Fb2itjU1@individual.net>`

```
Frank Swarbrick wrote:
>          IF VPAY-CARD-SVC                                    
>              IF DATE-OPEN < DATE-40-DAYS-AGO               
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>          END-IF                                          

This is an extension, I believe, that allows this to compile at all - an 
"If" terminated with an "End-If" isn't allowed to have a "NEXT SENTENCE" 
in it.  I did find that it could be "gotten around" by not terminating 
an inner IF...

IF CONDITION-IS-TRUE
     IF CONDITION-IS-TRUE
         PERFORM STUFF
     ELSE
         NEXT SENTENCE
ELSE
     DISPLAY "WHY WOULD ANYONE CODE THIS?"
END-IF

This will compile.  The usage in your original example is so easily 
broken, it's not even funny.  :)  Especially if you're in a shop that 
doesn't mandate one-period-per-paragraph...
```

```


>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>> Ursprüngliche Nachricht <<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<

Am 02.06.05, 16:24:00, schrieb "Frank Swarbrick" 
<Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com> zum Thema interesting use of NEXT 
SENTENCE vs. CONTINUE:

<snip>

> *    *************************************************************
>          IF VPAY-CARD-SVC
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>          GO TO PROCESS-LARGE-BAL-CHANGE.

<snip too>

Hello Frank

this is very tricky and expensive code - its FORBIDDEN - you found it 
and that is a good way to become good code.

Why forbidden: if you change the 'point' you can get an other 
solution. So you must establish some instance, to get right code. It 
is garbage :-) Another hint is, you can not change the code before you 
think more than one time. It is destructive for a 'comercial rigth' 
software development.

Oh oh if i can pronounce it in german :-( it come some times better

sowas ist absolute schei.... Nicht nur dass es Geld kostet. Es GEHOERT 
verboten!!!!


Einen schoenen Tag
Andreas Lerch

<a id="t-50d80ab6b3"></a>
## Thread: RANDOM vs. DYNAMIC on indexed output file — 10 msgs

### RANDOM vs. DYNAMIC on indexed output file

- **From:** "Frank Swarbrick" <Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com>
- **Date:** 2005-06-02T17:56:16-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3g9kl1Fbd2n0U1@individual.net>`

```
Is there any difference between opening as OUTPUT an indexed file with
ACCESS MODE RANDOM and ACCESS MODE DYNAMIC?

I know the difference when opening for INPUT or I-O, but I can't think of
how one of the above would be preferred over the other.

What brings this fairly silly question up is that I have a sorted input file
that I read in, extract some data from, and write the "modified" version of
some of the records to an indexed output file.  I had been opening the
output file as SEQUENTIAL, and this worked fine.  That is, until I needed to
add a "header" record.   I can't actually create the header record until
I've read all of the other records, so I put my logic to create the header
after I finished processing the input file.  This, of course, caused a write
error with status '21' (sequence error).

I'm not really looking for a solution, because I already have two:
1) Add a trailer record instead of a header record.
or
2) Define output file as ACCESS RANDOM instead of SEQUENTIAL.

But it made me wonder what difference it might make using ACCESS DYNAMIC
instead of RANDOM.
I tried both ways and DYNAMIC *seems* to run faster, but I don't know if
that's just because of CPU utilization issues or if it actually makes sense
that DYNAMIC would be faster because it would do SEQUENTIAL for 99% of the
records.

Frank


--- 
Frank Swarbrick
Senior Developer/Analyst - Mainframe Applications
FirstBank Data Corporation - Lakewood, CO  USA
```

#### ↳ Re: RANDOM vs. DYNAMIC on indexed output file

- **From:** "HeyBub" <heybubNOSPAM@gmail.com>
- **Date:** 2005-06-02T21:25:20-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<119vfscobpe0cbf@news.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<3g9kl1Fbd2n0U1@individual.net>`

```
Frank Swarbrick wrote:
> Is there any difference between opening as OUTPUT an indexed file with
> ACCESS MODE RANDOM and ACCESS MODE DYNAMIC?
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> with status '21' (sequence error).

Well, you actually CAN write the header record first. Write a dummy record - 
with the low key - then, when finished update it with record counts or 
whatever.


>
> I'm not really looking for a solution, because I already have two:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>

If you can't really tell the difference in your timing tests, then it 
probably doesn't make any difference.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: RANDOM vs. DYNAMIC on indexed output file

- **From:** "g z2" <gz2@libero.it>
- **Date:** 2005-06-03T15:40:13+02:00

- **Message-ID:** `<d7pmk4$73e$1@fata.cs.interbusiness.it>`

```
hi!

the best way is:
open output the indexed file
write sequentially all the file
close
open i-o
set the key and write the label record
close

doing so, the computer write all the index really in order and it's very 
fast.
and all the next read access will fly.
you can do it only if you declare the file for dynamic.

bye!
gz

"HeyBub" <heybubNOSPAM@gmail.com> wrote in message 
news:119vfscobpe0cbf@news.supernews.com...
> Frank Swarbrick wrote:
>> Is there any difference between opening as OUTPUT an indexed file with
…[36 more quoted lines elided]…

```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: RANDOM vs. DYNAMIC on indexed output file

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-06-03T19:17:08-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1117851428.115481.253950@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<d7pmk4$73e$1@fata.cs.interbusiness.it>`
- **References:** `<3g9kl1Fbd2n0U1@individual.net> <119vfscobpe0cbf@news.supernews.com> <d7pmk4$73e$1@fata.cs.interbusiness.it>`

```
> the computer write all the index really in order and it's very
> fast. and all the next read access will fly.
> you can do it only if you declare the file for dynamic.

Writing an indexed file in ascending key order is not necessarily the
best mechanism to get an efficient index.  Many b-tree indexes need to
be rotated to keep the tree balanced and an all ascending key will mean
that there will be the most number of rotations required.

I have seen some systems (eg early RM Cobol) where the index was never
rotated and loading a file in ascending key sequence resulted in the
worst possible index that was basically a single linked list.  In these
cases it was necessary to sort the records to a random sequence in
order that the index resulted in a reasonable balance.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: RANDOM vs. DYNAMIC on indexed output file

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Robert Jones" <rjones0@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2005-06-04T02:55:26-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1117878926.227936.129310@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<1117851428.115481.253950@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<3g9kl1Fbd2n0U1@individual.net> <119vfscobpe0cbf@news.supernews.com> <d7pmk4$73e$1@fata.cs.interbusiness.it> <1117851428.115481.253950@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`

```
This is a good reason for referring to the language reference manual
and programmers guide for the particular version of COBOL in use for
their recommendations.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: RANDOM vs. DYNAMIC on indexed output file

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "slade2" <jacksleight@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2005-06-04T14:47:52-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1117921672.367188.21360@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<1117878926.227936.129310@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<3g9kl1Fbd2n0U1@individual.net> <119vfscobpe0cbf@news.supernews.com> <d7pmk4$73e$1@fata.cs.interbusiness.it> <1117851428.115481.253950@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <1117878926.227936.129310@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com>`

```
Hi Frank,

In an IBM Mainframe environment the following applies.  I assume it's
the same for others.

With RANDOM only random access of recs is allowed; with SEQUENTIAL only
seq access of recs is allowed.

With DYNAMIC both modes of access is allowed.

Use READ NEXT when doing a seq read; use READ when doing a random read.

Just make sure the key field in the FD contains the value you need.
```

With DYNAMIC both modes of access is allowed.

Use READ NEXT when doing a seq read; use READ when doing a random read.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: RANDOM vs. DYNAMIC on indexed output file

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Frank Swarbrick" <Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com>
- **Date:** 2005-06-06T14:25:08-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3gjpp5Fcp7qiU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<3g9kl1Fbd2n0U1@individual.net> <119vfscobpe0cbf@news.supernews.com> <d7pmk4$73e$1@fata.cs.interbusiness.it> <1117851428.115481.253950@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`

```
My indexed file system is IBM VSAM/VSE, so I would guess (or hope!) that if
I declare the access as being sequential it would handle this in the most
efficient manner possible.  Now if I declared it as RANDOM or DYNAMIC I'm
not so sure, though DYNAMIC did appear to run faster than RANDOM.

In any case, I'm going to depend on the file system to work efficiently
until such time as I determine that it's not.

Thanks for the info, in any case.
Frank

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Disappearing data on screen

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-06-07T10:57:46+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3gk2nhFcs507U1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<1118012259.831407.79890@g43g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <3ghnffFcb28sU1@individual.net> <1118089192.398974.77920@g44g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`

```
Sorry for previous blank post...finger trouble.

See below...

"Colonel_Buck" <bkeith@alumni.utexas.net> wrote in message
news:1118089192.398974.77920@g44g2000cwa.googlegroups.com...
>
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>
You have missed the point of these constructs. (I don't blame you because
the concepts are not even mentioned in the manual).

TO allows you to protect fields so that they become 'read only'.  It is
intended to emulate devices in mainframe environments that have attributes
to do this (like IBM 3270).

Having decided that your field will be 'read only', it would be a
contradiction to then place output data in it. So FROM cannot work with TO.

In the same way, an output only field can be defined with FROM, and, in the
same way it will be incompatible with TO.(If the field is defined for output
only, you cannot accept input from it.)

USING allows both input and output of the field, as you have seen.

SUMMARY:
You may find it helpful to think of these constructs in the following way:

TO = input only. Will not be refreshed after data is collected from it.
FROM = output only. Will not allow input of data in the same field. The
field is refreshed when redisplayed.
USING = input/output. Allows data collection and can be updated. The field
is refreshed with its current contents on subsequent displays.

ADVICE: (Opinion that is arguable, but I have no intention of arguing it;
make up your own mind.)

Screen sections in COBOL, particularly in Client/Server environments, are an
obsolete cop out for people who can't deal with event driven GUI
programming, and are hanging desperately to the command line interface
because 'we've always done it that way'. COBOL programmers tend to think in
terms of procedural programming and are used to controlling the process, so
ACCEPT and DISPLAY is simple and familiar.

Get into event driven programming; it is fun and satisfying.

You have a superb environment for developing  'quick build' GUI
applications: PowerCOBOL. (It comes with Fujitsu NetCOBOL version 5).

Do the PowerCOBOL tutorial. You won't be sorry.

Pete.
```
